meta.description: Human origin story - played before prologue

human_001.title: Before the Fall
text: You remember the smell first. That's what they never tell you about rock bottom — it has a smell. Recycled air thick with ozone and sweat, the chemical tang of cheap synth-food, the iron bite of rust bleeding through walls that were never meant to last this long. You breathe it in and you know: this is where people go to be forgotten.

The intake officer at the Lower District Housing Bureau didn't look at you when she processed your application. She didn't need to. You were a number, a biometric scan, a credit rating that barely qualified you for a coffin-sized room in Sector 7. She stamped the form, slid a keycard across the counter, and said the same thing she says to every new arrival: 'Don't die in the hallways. Cleanup costs come out of your deposit.'

But you weren't always here. You weren't always this. There was a before — a life, a name that meant something, people who would have recognized your face. That before feels like a dream now, fading at the edges, but it happened. It was real. And how you got from there to here is a story written in scar tissue and regret.

choices.text: You grew up on these streets. A rat among rats.
You were a soldier once. You had a uniform and a purpose.
You escaped from a corporate laboratory. Your body still carries the marks.
You sold yourself to pay a debt. The debt is paid. You are what's left.

human_002.title: Street Rat — The Alley
text: Your first memory is an alley. Not a crib, not a mother's face, not a lullaby. An alley in Sector 4 where the drainage pipes leaked warm water that kept you from freezing through the winter. You were maybe four. Maybe five. Age is a luxury for people with birth certificates.

The other children called you Mouse because you were small and fast and could squeeze through gaps in security fencing that the bigger kids couldn't. You learned to steal before you learned to read. A synth-fruit here, a credit chip there. The vendors in the market district would chase you with shock-batons if they caught you, but they rarely caught you. You were quick. You were invisible. You were nothing.

By the time you were ten, you had joined a crew. Not a gang — gangs had territory and hierarchy and rules. A crew was just a handful of kids who watched each other's backs because nobody else would. There was Ticker, who had a bad heart and a worse cough. Sera, who could pick any lock manufactured before 2145. And Dace, who was bigger than all of you and carried a piece of rebar like a sword.

choices.text: The crew was family. The only one you ever had.
You kept your distance even from them. Trust gets you killed.

human_003.title: Street Rat — Family of Strays
text: You shared everything. The food you stole, the blankets you scavenged, the warmth of a chemical fire burning in a trash drum on nights when the temperature dropped below what baseline human bodies were designed to endure. Ticker would tell stories he'd overheard from bar patrons — wild tales about the upper city, where people ate real food and the air tasted clean. Sera would demonstrate her lockpicking on anything with a mechanism, her small fingers working with a surgeon's precision. Dace would keep watch, his silhouette a reassuring shadow against the alley mouth.

It wasn't love. You didn't have a word for it then. It was just the desperate arithmetic of survival: alone you die, together you might not. But looking back, you know it was the closest thing to love you'd ever felt.
Then the winter of '48 came, and Ticker's cough got worse.

choices.text: You tried to get medicine for Ticker. You stole from the wrong people.
You couldn't do anything. You just watched him get sicker.

human_004.title: Street Rat — The Lone Wolf
text: You ran with them but you slept alone. You ate with them but you kept your own stash hidden in a vent three blocks away. You watched Ticker's cough get worse through the autumn and felt nothing except the cold calculation that a sick member slows the crew down. That's what the streets teach you: sentiment is a tax on survival, and you couldn't afford it.

Sera noticed. She always noticed things. One night she found you checking your stash and stood in the mouth of the vent shaft with her arms crossed, her face unreadable in the dark. 'You don't trust us,' she said. Not a question. 'I don't trust anyone,' you replied. She was quiet for a long time. Then: 'That's going to be a problem someday.'
She was right. But not in the way either of you expected.

choices.text: The crew got caught on a job. You had a choice to make.
You eventually left the crew before things went bad.

human_005.title: Street Rat — The Pharmacy Job
text: The med-dispensary on Seventh and Crucible was a fortress. Reinforced shutters, biometric locks, two armed guards who rotated in four-hour shifts. It serviced the mid-level workers who could afford basic healthcare — antibiotics, pain patches, the cheap immuno-boosters that kept the plagues at bay. For someone like Ticker, who needed a bronchial stabilizer that cost more than your crew earned in a month, it might as well have been on the moon.

But you found a way in. A drainage access point behind the building that connected to the ventilation system. You were small enough. You were desperate enough. And for two nights you cased the building, memorizing guard rotations, counting the seconds between security sweeps, until you had a window: ninety seconds between the camera resets.
Sera said it was suicide. Dace said nothing, just tightened his grip on his rebar. Ticker coughed blood into a rag and tried to tell you not to bother.
You went anyway.

choices.text: You pulled it off. But the guards saw your face.
You got caught inside. They beat you and threw you out.

human_006.title: Street Rat — Watching Ticker Fade
text: It took three weeks. Three weeks of listening to him gasp and rattle through the night, of watching the color drain from his skin until he looked like something already dead. The free clinic in Sector 3 turned him away — no registration, no treatment, that's the policy, come back with a valid ID or don't come back at all. You carried him there and you carried him home and somewhere on that walk back you felt something inside you calcify into a thing harder than grief.

Ticker died on a Tuesday. You remember because it was trash collection day and the grinding of the compactor trucks drowned out the sound of Sera crying. Dace dug a hole in the chemical waste lot behind the processing plant, and you buried him wrapped in the thermal blanket he'd slept in every night since you'd known him. No words. None of you knew any prayers. Dace just drove his rebar into the ground as a marker, and you stood there in the acid rain until your skin burned.
You were twelve years old.

choices.text: After Ticker, you swore you'd get out. Whatever it took.
After Ticker, something shut off inside you. You became harder.

human_007.title: Street Rat — The Choice
text: The job was simple: lift a crate of synth-stim from a delivery truck while Dace kept the driver talking. Sera would pop the lock. You'd be gone in thirty seconds. But the truck belonged to the Neon Court, and nobody told you that because nobody knew, and when the enforcers materialized from the alley with shock-prods and that dead-eyed corporate calm, the plan went sideways in the time it takes a heart to beat.

Dace went down first. A shock-prod to the base of his skull, and he folded like his strings were cut. Sera ran. You heard her footsteps receding into the maze of market stalls, getting fainter. Two enforcers gave chase. The third stood over Dace's body and looked at you.
'Run and we find you,' the enforcer said. 'Stay and talk, maybe your friend keeps his kneecaps.'
You could hear Sera's footsteps almost gone. You could see Dace on the ground, breathing but broken.

choices.text: Stay. Give them what they want. Protect Dace.
Run. Dace would understand. Survival first.

human_008.title: Street Rat — Walking Away
text: You left on a night when Ticker's cough was so bad he couldn't sleep and Sera was picking the lock on a door she had no business opening and Dace was sharpening his rebar against a cinder block with a sound like bones breaking. You took your stash from the vent, slung your bag over your shoulder, and walked into the dark without a word.

Nobody followed. Maybe they didn't notice. Maybe they'd been expecting it. You'd never been all the way in, and crew instincts are sharp enough to sense the difference between a member and a passenger.

You spent the next three years alone. You learned to sleep light, eat less, trust nothing. The streets are a different kind of teacher when you're a solo act — every lesson comes with bruises, and the final exam is whether you wake up in the morning. But you survived. You always survived. That was your only real talent.

By sixteen you'd carved out a territory of sorts: a stretch of maintenance tunnels beneath the transit hub where nobody went because the radiation readings were just high enough to scare the cautious. You weren't cautious. You were tired.

choices.text: You built something in those tunnels. A life, of sorts.
Walking away was the smart play. You don't regret it.

human_009.title: Street Rat — The Heist Succeeds
text: Ninety seconds. You counted every one of them in the dark, your hands shaking as you pried open the dispensary cabinet and grabbed every vial of bronchial stabilizer you could carry. The vent shaft was tight enough to scrape skin from your shoulders, and when you emerged into the alley behind the building you were bleeding from a dozen places and clutching a bag of stolen medicine like it was the only thing in the world that mattered. Because it was.

Ticker took the stabilizer and for two weeks he got better. Color came back to his cheeks. The cough softened. He laughed again, this rattling wet sound that was ugly and beautiful at the same time. You'd done it. You'd beaten the system.

But the guards had seen your face. The dispensary's security feed caught you on the way out — blurry, partial, but enough. They posted your image on the bounty boards: WANTED FOR PHARMACEUTICAL THEFT. REWARD: 500 CREDITS. It wasn't much, but in the Lower District, 500 credits was enough to make every desperate soul a potential informant.
Your crew had to move. And move again. And again.

choices.text: The running couldn't last forever. Eventually they found you.
[Dexterity 11] Run faster. You've always been fast.

human_010.title: Street Rat — Caught and Broken
text: The guard was waiting on the other side of the vent. He'd heard you coming — your breathing too loud, your body scraping against the metal — and when you emerged he grabbed you by the collar and dragged you into the light like a rat pulled from a wall. The second guard hit you twice. Once in the stomach, once across the jaw. You felt a tooth loosen. Blood filled your mouth with its copper warmth.

They threw you into the alley. No arrest — you were too young, too unregistered, too worthless to process. Just a beating and a warning: 'Come back and we break your hands. Then you won't steal anything ever again.'

You crawled back to the crew. Sera cleaned the blood off your face with a rag that smelled like engine grease. Dace looked at you with something that might have been respect or might have been pity. Ticker coughed and said nothing.
He died two weeks later. And you knew, with the quiet certainty of a child who has learned the weight of failure, that you had let him down.

choices.text: Ticker's death changed you. You had to become something harder.
[Strength 10] Become harder. Become someone they fear.

human_011.title: Street Rat — The Vow
text: You started taking bigger risks. Not out of recklessness, but out of a cold, deliberate fury that settled into your bones like lead. The Lower District was a machine built to grind people like you into paste, and you refused to be paste. You learned the systems: which vendors left their stock unguarded during shift changes, which security patrols had gaps you could exploit, which mid-level workers could be bribed or blackmailed into looking the other way.

By fourteen you were running jobs solo, building a reputation as someone who could get things — medicine, tech, information, access. The street name they gave you was Whisper, because you moved through the district like a rumor: everyone heard about you, nobody saw you.
But reputation is a double-edged blade in the Lower District. The same visibility that brought you clients also brought you to the attention of people with more power and fewer scruples.

choices.text: A fixer named Greel offered you steady work. You took it.
You tried to go legitimate. Get a real job. Be normal.

human_012.title: Street Rat — Calcification
text: Grief is a luxury. That's what you told yourself as you walked away from the chemical waste lot where Ticker was buried, the acid rain eating tiny holes in your jacket. Grief is for people with the time and safety to feel it. You had neither. So you took everything soft inside you and compressed it down, down, down until it was a diamond-hard point at the center of your chest, and you built walls around it, and you moved on.

You started fighting. Not organized, not for money at first — just alley scraps with other street kids over territory, food, shelter. You discovered that anger, properly directed, is a kind of fuel. You weren't the biggest or the strongest, but you were the most willing to keep going after you should have stopped, and that willingness terrified people who had more to lose.

Sera drifted away. Said you scared her now. Said you looked at people like they were problems to solve, not humans to know. Dace stayed. Dace understood. You and Dace built something together over the next few years — not a crew, not exactly. A partnership. Two hard things grinding against a harder world.

choices.text: Dace got killed in a turf war. You were alone again.
You and Dace took a job that went wrong. Very wrong.

human_013.title: Street Rat — Staying
text: You stayed. You let the enforcer grab you by the arm and drag you under the light while the other two carried Dace to their transport. They asked you questions you mostly couldn't answer — who sent you, what organization you worked for, who your handler was. You were a child. You didn't have a handler. You had a dying friend and empty pockets and a stomach that hadn't been full in three days.

They hit you when your answers weren't useful. Open-palmed strikes that rattled your vision and left your ears ringing. Professional violence, administered with the bored efficiency of someone filling out paperwork. When they were done, they dumped both of you in an alley in Sector 6, four miles from anywhere you knew.

Dace had a fractured cheekbone and two broken ribs. You had a split lip, a loosened tooth, and a lesson burned into your memory: the powerful don't punish you because you matter. They punish you because it costs them nothing.
But Dace was alive. You'd made a choice, and Dace was alive.

choices.text: That loyalty defined you. You'd do it again.
Loyalty is a weakness. You know that now.

human_014.title: Street Rat — Running
text: Your legs moved before your conscience could object. You bolted into the market maze, ducking under stalls, vaulting over crates, your heart hammering so loud it drowned out everything except the rhythm of your own desperate flight. The enforcers didn't chase you. You weren't worth the effort. You were a street kid, disposable, interchangeable with a hundred others. They had Dace, and Dace was enough.

You found Sera three hours later, hiding in a drainage culvert, her lockpicking tools clutched to her chest like a rosary. 'Where's Dace?' she asked. You couldn't answer. You couldn't even look at her.

You never saw Dace again. Months later, a rumor filtered through the street network: a kid matching his description had been picked up by Meridian Sec-Corp for an enforced labor contract. Fourteen years old and already property. The Neon Court's way of recouping their losses — steal from us, we steal you.
Sera left after that. Said she couldn't look at you without seeing the choice you made. You didn't argue. She was right.

choices.text: The guilt never left. But it taught you to be faster, smarter, harder to catch.
[Dexterity 10] You ran. You survived. That's what matters.

human_015.title: Street Rat — The Tunnels
text: The maintenance tunnels beneath the transit hub became your kingdom. A kingdom of corroded pipes and flickering emergency lights and the constant subterranean hum of the city's circulatory system moving cargo and waste and people through its concrete arteries. The radiation was real but low — enough to keep away the casual squatter, not enough to kill you. Probably. The free clinic's dosimeter readings were always borderline, which meant either you were fine or you were dying so slowly it didn't matter yet.

You furnished the space with scavenged materials: a mattress from a condemned building, a portable heater jury-rigged from a broken industrial unit, a water filter that turned the brown trickle from the pipes into something that wouldn't make you immediately sick. You stole a terminal from a recycling depot and taught yourself to read on it, then to code, then to access restricted networks. Information was the real currency down here, and you were building a vault.

By seventeen you were a ghost in the machine. You knew the tunnel networks better than the engineers who built them. You knew which conduits carried corporate data and which ones carried power. You knew how to tap into both without being detected.

choices.text: Then the corps found your tunnel. They always find you eventually.
You used your network access to pull a job that changed everything.

human_016.title: Street Rat — The Fixer
text: Greel ran his operation out of a bar called The Rust Bucket in Sector 7. The name was generous — it was less a bar and more a hole in a wall where someone had installed a counter and a still that produced liquor capable of stripping paint. Greel himself was a wiry man with chrome teeth and eyes that never stopped calculating. He'd been running fixers in the Lower District for twenty years, and he'd survived by being smart enough to stay small and ruthless enough to stay alive.

'I've been hearing your name,' he said, pouring you a drink you didn't ask for. 'Or rather, not hearing it. That's the trick, isn't it? Being useful enough to be noticed, invisible enough not to be caught.' He pushed the glass toward you. 'I've got work. Regular work. The kind that pays in credits instead of bruises.'

The work was simple at first. Deliveries. Information drops. Packages moved from A to B without questions about what was inside. You were good at it because you knew the streets like your own heartbeat and because you'd spent your entire life being overlooked.

choices.text: Greel's jobs got bigger. And more dangerous.
You discovered Greel was working for the Neon Court. You confronted him.

human_017.title: Street Rat — The Legitimate Life
text: You got a job at the Foundry. Assembly line work, twelve-hour shifts, fitting circuit boards into chassis frames until your fingers bled and your vision swam. The pay was barely enough to cover a shared dormitory bunk and two meals of synth-paste a day. But it was legal. It was above board. For the first time in your life, you had a schedule, a supervisor, a paycheck that came in on a regular cycle.

It lasted four months. Four months of mind-numbing repetition, of being screamed at by a foreman who called you 'unit' instead of your name, of watching your coworkers shuffle through their shifts with the dead-eyed compliance of people who'd had the fight beaten out of them years ago. You tried. You really tried. You told yourself this was better than stealing, better than running, better than the alley. And maybe it was. But it was also a different kind of death — the slow one, the one that kills you by inches while you're still breathing.

On the last day, the foreman docked your pay for a bathroom break that ran two minutes over the allotted time. You looked at the reduced number on your credit chip and something in you snapped quietly, like a wire pulled too tight.

choices.text: You walked out. Back to the streets. At least the streets were honest about what they were.
You stole from the Foundry on your way out. Parts, credits, anything you could carry.

human_018.title: Street Rat — Dace Falls
text: The turf war was over nothing. It was always over nothing. Two crews fighting for control of a drainage tunnel that connected Sector 5 to Sector 6 — valuable real estate in the underground economy, but the kind of thing that shouldn't cost a life. Shouldn't. But this is the Lower District, and the distance between shouldn't and does is measured in bodies.

Dace took a blade between the ribs on a Tuesday night in the rain. You were twenty feet away, fighting someone else, and you heard him make a sound that wasn't a scream — it was quieter than that, more surprised. Like the knife was an unexpected answer to a question he'd never asked. By the time you reached him, he was sitting against a wall with his hands pressed to his side and blood running between his fingers like dark water.
'Hey,' he said. Just 'hey.' And then his eyes went somewhere you couldn't follow.

You sat with his body until the rain washed most of the blood away. Then you stood up, picked up his rebar, and walked into the dark with it. You didn't cry. You'd lost that ability years ago. But something in your chest cavity creaked like an overloaded support beam, and you knew that if it broke, everything above it would come down.

choices.text: After Dace, you had nothing left to lose. You took dangerous work.
[Wisdom 10] After Dace, you learned who to trust. No one.

human_019.title: Street Rat — Displacement
text: They cleared the tunnels with industrial fumigation drones. Non-lethal, technically — the gas was designed to incapacitate, not kill. But 'non-lethal' is a term defined by people who've never been on the receiving end. You came to in an alley three blocks from the transit hub, coughing blood-flecked foam, your eyes burning, your skin covered in a chemical rash that took weeks to heal. Everything you'd built in the tunnels — the terminal, the heater, the water filter, the careful network of tapped data lines — was gone.

The displacement notice was posted on every wall in Sector 7: BY ORDER OF MERIDIAN INFRASTRUCTURE DIVISION, ALL UNAUTHORIZED OCCUPANTS OF SUB-LEVEL MAINTENANCE CORRIDORS ARE SUBJECT TO REMOVAL AND PROSECUTION. Prosecution meant labor camps. Removal meant what had just happened to you.

You were back to nothing. Again. The streets had taken everything from you so many times that you'd lost count, but this time felt different. This time you were older, and tiredness had replaced anger as the dominant frequency of your existence.

choices.text: You found Greel. Took whatever work he had. You had no choice.
Greel is dangerous. But so is starving.

human_020.title: Ex-Soldier — Enlistment
text: You signed up at seventeen because the alternative was starving. That's the recruitment pitch they don't put on the posters — the ones with the clean-jawed soldiers standing in front of billowing flags and gleaming dropships. SERVE THE CITY. PROTECT THE PEACE. EARN YOUR FUTURE. What they don't mention is that the enlistment office is always in the poorest sector, that the recruiters know exactly how many meals you've missed this week, and that the contract is binding for six years with penalties for early termination that would bankrupt a mid-level executive.

You passed the physical. Barely. Underweight, malnourished, with the kind of dense wiry muscle that comes from a lifetime of running and fighting and sleeping cold. The medical officer noted your condition with clinical detachment: 'Baseline human, no augmentations, moderate malnutrition, suitable for infantry conditioning.' Suitable. That was the first time anyone had ever called you suitable for anything.

They gave you a uniform, a bunk, and three meals a day. For the first week, you couldn't sleep because the bed was too soft. You'd spent so long on concrete and metal that the mattress felt like a trap.

choices.text: Basic training broke you down and built you back up. You thrived.
You struggled. The discipline was harder than the streets in its own way.

human_021.title: Ex-Soldier — Thriving
text: You discovered something in basic training that surprised you: you were good at this. The physical conditioning was brutal — eighteen-hour days of running, climbing, fighting, shooting — but your body had been preparing for it your entire life without knowing it. Years of hunger had made you efficient. Years of violence had made you fast. Years of sleeping with one eye open had given you reflexes that the drill instructors noticed and the other recruits envied.

By week six, you were squad leader. By month three, you'd earned the nickname 'Granite' because nothing the instructors threw at you seemed to crack your surface. They didn't know that you'd already been broken and rebuilt so many times that the process was familiar. They mistook durability for toughness. You let them.

Your commanding officer, Captain Vasquez, pulled you aside after a training exercise where your squad completed an urban assault course in record time. She was a tall woman with a prosthetic jaw and eyes that had seen things she never talked about. 'You've got instincts,' she said. 'Raw ones. Unpolished. But the raw ones are the ones that keep you alive.'
She recommended you for advanced combat training. Specialized unit. Higher clearance. Better pay.

choices.text: You accepted. You wanted to be the best.
You hesitated. More training meant more commitment to a system you didn't fully trust.

human_022.title: Ex-Soldier — Struggling
text: The streets teach you to survive. The military teaches you to obey. These are not the same thing, and the gap between them nearly destroyed you. You couldn't follow orders without questioning them. You couldn't stand in formation without scanning for exits. You flinched at sudden sounds that the other recruits shrugged off, and you didn't flinch at things that should have scared you, and the drill instructor marked both behaviors as problems.

Three weeks in, you got into a fight with another recruit — a corporate kid from the mid-levels who called you 'gutter trash' during a sparring exercise. You broke his nose, his orbital bone, and three of his fingers before they pulled you off. The disciplinary hearing was brief. The tribunal's verdict: six weeks of confined quarters and a formal reprimand on your record. They almost discharged you. Captain Vasquez intervened.

'You've got a problem with authority,' she told you through the cell's intercom. 'That's fine. So did I. But you need to learn to hide it better, or this machine will chew you up and spit out the parts it can't use.'

choices.text: You learned to hide it. You adapted. You survived.
You never fully adapted. But you served because there was nothing else.

human_023.title: Ex-Soldier — The Unit
text: They called it Echo Company. Twelve soldiers, handpicked from the general infantry for skills that didn't fit neatly into standard operational categories. Your squad was deployed to the outer perimeter zones — the contested territories beyond the city walls where corporate interests clashed with wildland settlements and mutant enclaves and the crumbling remains of the old world.

Your unit was a collection of damaged people given guns and a purpose. Kovac, the demolitions expert who hummed lullabies while assembling charges. Pin, the sniper whose hands shook unless she was looking through a scope. Tanaka, the medic who'd been discharged from a corporate hospital for refusing to let a patient die because their insurance lapsed. And Vasquez, who led from the front and never asked you to do anything she wouldn't do herself.

For three years, Echo Company was your life. You ran missions in zones that didn't appear on any civilian map. You saw things that the city's news feeds would never report. And somewhere in the dust and blood and adrenaline, you found something you'd never had before: a place where you belonged.

choices.text: Then came the Ashfall mission. The one that changed everything.
[Intelligence 10] Study the mission briefing. Something doesn't add up.

human_024.title: Ex-Soldier — The Reluctant Soldier
text: You served in a standard infantry platoon. The work was what you expected — patrols, checkpoints, crowd control in the lower sectors during unrest cycles. You weren't a hero. You weren't a disaster. You were a body in a uniform, filling a slot in a roster, doing what you were told with just enough competence to avoid attention and just enough rebellion to maintain your sanity.

The soldiers around you were a cross-section of the city's discarded: debt-defaulters working off their balance, street kids like you who'd traded one kind of cage for another, a few true believers who actually thought they were making a difference. You watched the true believers with a mixture of envy and contempt. Must be nice, you thought, to believe in something enough to die for it.

Two years in, your platoon was reassigned to security detail for a corporate mining operation in the wastes outside the city. Routine work, they said. Low risk. Standard hazard pay. What they didn't say was that the miners were indentured laborers — debt slaves — and your job was to make sure they didn't run.

choices.text: You did your job. You guarded the miners. You tried not to think about it.
You couldn't stomach it. You helped some of them escape.

human_025.title: Ex-Soldier — Ashfall
text: The briefing called it a 'pacification operation.' A settlement called Ashfall, population three hundred, located in the contaminated zone east of the city. Intelligence claimed the settlement was harboring insurgents who'd been raiding corporate supply convoys. Echo Company was to neutralize the threat and secure the area for industrial reclamation.

You went in at dawn. The settlement was a collection of salvaged structures built around a dead reactor core — people living in the literal shadow of the thing that had poisoned them. The inhabitants were gaunt, irradiated, many showing signs of gene-plague mutation. They were not soldiers. They were farmers and scavengers trying to grow food in toxic soil and raise children who might live past thirty.

There were no insurgents. The 'intelligence' was manufactured. The corporate entity that wanted the land — Helion Industries — had fabricated the threat assessment to justify the removal of an inconvenient population sitting on top of a valuable mineral deposit.
By the time your unit realized the truth, it was too late. The other squads had already begun the operation.

choices.text: You followed orders. You'll never forgive yourself.
You refused the order. Vasquez backed you up.
You tried to warn the settlers. Chaos erupted.

human_026.title: Ex-Soldier — The Guard
text: You stood on the perimeter and watched the miners work. Twelve-hour shifts in the heat, breaking rocks with tools that were older than the people using them. The dust got into everything — lungs, eyes, food, water. The miners coughed the same cough Ticker had, and at night they slept in barracks that were locked from the outside. You held the key.

There was a woman named Asha who worked the north face of the quarry. She was maybe forty but looked sixty, with hands like leather and eyes that still held a spark of defiance that the others had lost. She spoke to you once, during a water break, while you stood with your rifle and pretended not to see the blood on her palms.

'Do you know the difference between a slave and a prisoner?' she asked. You didn't answer. 'A prisoner knows there's a world outside the walls. A slave forgets.' She looked at your uniform, at the corporate logo stitched on your shoulder. 'Which are you?'
You didn't have an answer then. You do now.

choices.text: Something happened at the mine that ended your service. An incident.
[Strength 11] The incident at the mine ended your career but not your fight.

human_027.title: Ex-Soldier — The Escape
text: You disabled the lock on the north barracks at 0300. Six miners slipped out into the dark, Asha among them. You'd mapped a route through the perimeter sensor grid — a gap caused by a faulty relay that maintenance hadn't fixed because the budget request was still processing. Bureaucracy as liberation tool. The irony wasn't lost on you.

They made it. All six of them vanished into the wastes like ghosts, and you went back to your bunk and lay there in the dark, your heart hammering, waiting for the alarm that would end your life as you knew it.

It came at dawn. The empty barracks. The disabled lock. The investigation was swift and thorough. They didn't have proof it was you — you'd been careful — but they had suspicion, which in the military is almost as damning. Your squad leader looked at you with eyes that said I know, and you looked back with eyes that said I'd do it again.
They transferred you to a punishment detail. Six months of latrine duty and perimeter patrols in the most irradiated zones, the ones where the dosimeter clicks so fast it sounds like rain.

choices.text: The punishment detail was where you met the person who would change everything.
The punishment detail was hell. But hell has its lessons.

human_028.title: Ex-Soldier — Following Orders
text: You cleared buildings. That's the clinical term for it. You went room by room through the settlement with your weapon raised and you cleared buildings, and the people inside those buildings screamed or ran or begged or fought, and you did what you were trained to do because training is a kind of programming and when the adrenaline hits and the orders come through your earpiece, the programming takes over and the human being steps aside.

Kovac set charges on the settlement's water purifier. Pin covered the north exit. Tanaka treated the wounded — theirs and yours — with the same mechanical efficiency. And Vasquez stood in the center of it all with her jaw set and her eyes empty, directing the operation with a voice that never wavered.
It was over in two hours. The settlement was destroyed. The survivors were relocated to processing centers. The mineral deposit was secured. Mission accomplished.

Tanaka put in for a transfer the next day. Pin stopped talking. Kovac kept humming his lullabies, but the melody changed to something you didn't recognize. And you — you looked at your hands and understood that they would never be clean again.

choices.text: Ashfall haunted you. It was why you eventually left.
Orders are orders. The guilt came later.

human_029.title: Ex-Soldier — Refusing the Order
text: 'Negative.' The word came out of you before you knew you were going to say it. Your squad was stacked at the entrance of a residential structure — a family inside, you could hear a child crying — and the order in your earpiece was clear and unambiguous and you said 'negative' and your body stopped moving forward.

Vasquez looked at you. The rest of the squad looked at you. In the military, refusing an order in the field is grounds for immediate detention or, in combat zones, summary execution. The silence stretched like a wire about to snap.

'Confirmed,' Vasquez said. Just that one word. She keyed her comm. 'Echo Lead to command. Resistance heavier than anticipated. Civilian presence confirmed. Requesting revised ROE.' There was no heavier resistance. There were no combatants. Vasquez was lying to command to buy time, and everyone on the comm channel knew it.

Echo Company was pulled from the operation. Another unit finished what you wouldn't. The result was the same. The settlement fell. The people were displaced or worse. But your hands were clean — or at least cleaner — and Vasquez had put her career on the line to keep them that way.

choices.text: The fallout from Ashfall ended Echo Company.
[Charisma 10] Refusing wasn't enough. You tried to stop the others too.

human_030.title: Ex-Soldier — Warning the Settlers
text: You broke formation. That's the part they'll write in your court-martial file: 'Subject broke formation during active operation and made unauthorized contact with civilian targets.' What the file won't say is that you ran to the nearest structure and hammered on the door and shouted 'RUN' in a voice that cracked with desperation, and a woman opened the door holding an infant, and you looked into her eyes and saw the same terror you'd seen in Ticker's eyes and Dace's eyes and every pair of eyes that ever looked at you from the wrong side of a power imbalance.

Chaos. That's the only word for what happened next. Settlers running in every direction, Echo Company trying to contain a situation that had exploded beyond containment, gunfire and screaming and the chemical stink of smoke grenades. Some of them made it out. Many didn't. You don't know the exact numbers because after the first shot was fired, the fog of war swallowed everything in a haze of noise and violence.
They arrested you on the field. Two soldiers held you face-down in the ash while a lieutenant screamed things you couldn't hear over the ringing in your ears.

choices.text: The court-martial was a formality. You already knew how it would end.
[Charisma 11] Face the tribunal with dignity.

human_031.title: Ex-Soldier — The Mine Incident
text: The collapse happened on a Thursday. A support structure in the east tunnel failed — substandard materials, deferred maintenance, the usual corporate calculus of profit over safety. Forty miners trapped under a hundred tons of rock and dirt. The rescue operation was... selective. The mining company sent engineers to assess structural damage to the equipment. The miners were an afterthought.

You went in anyway. Against orders, against protocol, against every survival instinct screaming at you to stay on the surface. You and two other soldiers from your platoon crawled into the unstable tunnel with flashlights and ropes and pulled eleven miners out of the rubble. Eleven out of forty. Asha wasn't among the survivors.

The company thanked you publicly and reprimanded you privately. Heroism that embarrasses the chain of command isn't rewarded — it's punished, quietly, through reassignments and denied promotions and performance reviews that damn with faint praise.

Your service record after the mine incident reads like a slow erasure. They didn't discharge you. They just made sure you'd never advance, never matter, never be in a position to embarrass them again.

choices.text: Your contract expired. They didn't renew it. You were free — and lost.
Freedom isn't free. It's just a different kind of prison.

human_032.title: Ex-Soldier — The Punishment Detail
text: The punishment detail was six months of hell in the irradiated outer zones. You patrolled dead ground where nothing grew and the air tasted like metal and the sunsets were beautiful in a poisoned, chemical way that made you think about endings. The other soldiers on the detail were like you — the inconvenient ones, the ones who'd asked questions or made mistakes or simply rubbed the wrong officer the wrong way.

Among them was a combat medic named Lira Chen. She'd been reassigned for filing an official complaint about inadequate medical supplies. Bureaucratic revenge for having standards. She was quiet, methodical, and angry in a way that burned clean instead of hot. She read actual books — physical ones, paper and ink — and at night she'd sit by the perimeter light and turn pages while the radiation clicks provided a metronomic backdrop.

'Why'd they send you here?' she asked one evening. You told her about the miners. She listened without judgment. Then she said: 'In a just system, you'd be decorated. In this one, you're here. That tells you everything you need to know about the system.'
You spent six months on that detail, and in those six months Lira Chen taught you more about the world than the military had in years.

choices.text: You fell in love with Lira. It was the first gentle thing in your life.
Lira became your closest friend. The only person who understood.

human_033.title: Ex-Soldier — The Aftermath
text: Echo Company was dissolved. That's the official version. The reality was messier: Vasquez was court-martialed for insubordination. Kovac was transferred to a logistics unit where his demolitions expertise would never be needed. Pin vanished into the intelligence division — they valued her silence. Tanaka left the military entirely and opened a free clinic in Sector 3 that would later turn you away because you didn't have valid ID.

And you — you served out the remainder of your contract in a haze of routine and regret. Guard duty. Patrol rotations. The numbing administrative machinery of a military that didn't know what to do with a soldier who'd seen too much and said too little. You didn't fight it. You didn't fight anything. Ashfall had used up your fight, spent it like currency in a transaction you never agreed to.

When your discharge papers came through, the officer who handed them to you said 'Thank you for your service' with the practiced insincerity of someone reading a script. You walked out of the base with a bag over your shoulder and nowhere to go.

choices.text: You drifted. Took odd jobs. Watched yourself slowly disappear.
[Wisdom 10] Stop drifting. Make a plan.

human_034.title: Ex-Soldier — Court-Martial
text: The trial lasted two days. The charges: desertion of post during active operations, unauthorized contact with designated targets, conduct unbecoming a soldier of the Municipal Defense Force. The evidence: your own squad's testimony, helmet-cam footage, comm logs. You didn't contest any of it.

Your appointed defense counsel — a tired-looking lieutenant who clearly wanted to be anywhere else — entered a plea of extenuating circumstances. He cited the false intelligence, the civilian presence, the absence of legitimate military targets. The tribunal listened with the polite indifference of people who'd already decided the outcome.

Dishonorable discharge. Three months in a military detention facility. Permanent black mark on your citizen record that would follow you into every background check, every job application, every interaction with authority for the rest of your life. The judge called it 'lenient.' She said you were lucky they weren't charging you with treason.

Lucky. In the detention cell, you turned the word over in your mind like a stone with sharp edges. Lucky. You'd tried to save people and they called you lucky for not being executed. The logic of the system was flawless in its brutality.

choices.text: Three months later, you walked out with nothing. Less than nothing.
Three months of nothing. Then the Lower District.

human_035.title: Ex-Soldier — Drifting
text: The city doesn't have a place for ex-soldiers. That's the thing nobody tells you when you sign the contract: the skills they teach you — killing, surviving, following orders — don't translate to civilian life. There's no job listing that says 'WANTED: person who can clear a room in four seconds and sleep through artillery fire. Must be comfortable with moral injury.'

You drifted through the lower sectors like debris in a current. Bouncer work at bars that couldn't afford real security. Day labor at construction sites that paid cash and didn't ask questions. A brief, disastrous stint as a bodyguard for a mid-level drug dealer who got killed by his own partners three weeks into your employment.

The money was never enough. The rooms got smaller. The meals got further apart. And at night, lying on whatever surface you were sleeping on that week, you'd hear the sounds again — the screaming, the gunfire, the crying of a child in a building you were ordered to clear — and you'd lie there in the dark with your fists clenched and your teeth grinding and wait for morning to come and replace one kind of hell with another.

choices.text: Then you met someone from your past. Someone who needed help.
You hit bottom. The absolute, concrete floor of human existence.

human_036.title: Ex-Soldier — Love in the Wasteland
text: It happened slowly, like weather. A hand brushing yours as she passed you a water canister. A laugh that sounded foreign in the irradiated quiet — your laugh, rusty from disuse. Late nights talking about the world as it was and the world as it should be, her voice steady and certain against the clicking of the dosimeters.

Lira kissed you on the fifty-seventh night. You know the number because you'd been counting, because counting was the only way to organize the chaos of what you were feeling. She tasted like the iron tablets she took for the radiation, and her hands were warm on the sides of your face, and for one crystalline moment the world contracted to a point of light that contained nothing except the two of you and the possibility that things could be different.

You loved her. You loved her with the desperate, bewildered gratitude of someone who'd been alone so long they'd forgotten that loneliness was a condition and not an identity. And she loved you back in her own way — fiercely, practically, with the clear-eyed tenderness of someone who knew that love in a wasteland is an act of defiance.

choices.text: But the wasteland takes everything. It took Lira.
When your punishment ended, you were separated. Different postings. The military knows how to hurt you.

human_037.title: Ex-Soldier — A Friend
text: Friendship is different from love and in some ways more durable. What you had with Lira was the friendship of two people who recognized each other's damage and chose not to look away. She didn't try to fix you. You didn't try to fix her. You just existed in the same irradiated space and found that the dosimeter clicked a little less ominously when you weren't alone.

She taught you to read between the lines of military communiques. You taught her to throw a punch that actually landed. She shared her books; you shared your rations. The other soldiers on the punishment detail watched your friendship with the suspicious curiosity of people who'd forgotten what genuine connection looked like.

When the detail ended and reassignments came through, Lira was sent to a medical facility in Sector 12. You were sent to a holding pattern — no specific assignment, no clear purpose, just the military's way of filing you under 'pending' until they figured out what to do with you.

'Find me when you get out,' Lira said. She pressed a book into your hands — an actual paper book, dog-eared and radiation-yellowed. 'Read it. It's about a man who refuses to stay broken.' You never found out the title. The pages were too damaged.

choices.text: Your contract expired. You left to find her. You never did.
You never found her. But you found something else — yourself.

human_038.title: Ex-Soldier — Losing Lira
text: The radiation got her. Not all at once — it never works that way. It creeps in through the cracks in your defenses, accumulates in bone and blood, and waits. Lira started losing weight first, then her hair, then the color in her skin. She diagnosed herself with the clinical detachment of a medic who'd seen it a hundred times in others and never expected to see it in the mirror.

'Acute radiation syndrome,' she said, reading her own blood work on a stolen diagnostic pad. 'Cumulative exposure. The dosimeters are reading wrong — they've been miscalibrated. Everyone on this detail is getting double the reported dose.' She said it the way someone might report the weather: factual, distant, already moving past the shock to the practical implications.

She died three weeks before the detail ended. You held her hand in the med-bay while the monitors flatlined and the attending medic — a kid, barely twenty — looked at you with eyes that said I'm sorry in a language you couldn't hear anymore.
You buried her book with her. You never found out how it ended.

choices.text: After Lira, the military was just a cage. You left as soon as you could.
After Lira, nothing mattered. Until something did.

human_039.title: Ex-Soldier — Convergence Point
text: The military made you. Not in the way they intended — not the sharp, obedient tool they'd tried to forge — but in the way that fire makes charcoal. It burned away everything soft and left something hard and dark and useful. You know how to fight, how to move through hostile terrain, how to survive on nothing. These are the skills of a soldier, and they are the skills of the damned, and you carry them with you into the Lower District like weapons you can't put down.

There's a room in Sector 7. Someone told someone who told someone that there's a vacancy, and the landlord doesn't ask questions, and the rent is low enough that the handful of credits in your pocket will cover two months if you don't eat every day. It's not a home. It's a waypoint. A place to stop moving long enough to figure out what comes next.
If anything comes next.

choices.text: Find the room. Put your back against a wall.
A soldier finds rest. You have earned it.

human_040.title: Lab Escapee — The Facility
text: You don't remember entering the facility. That's by design — they wiped the intake memories along with everything else they didn't want you to keep. What you remember is waking up: the cold, the light, the sterile white ceiling tiles, and the voice. Always the voice, calm and feminine, piped through speakers you couldn't see: 'Good morning, Subject 17. Today we'll be testing your response to Protocol Amber. Please remain still.'

The facility had no name that you knew. The staff wore white coats with no identifying markers. The other subjects — you knew there were others because you could hear them through the walls, screaming sometimes, silent at others — were numbered, not named. You were 17. You had been 17 for as long as you could remember, which wasn't very long, because memory was one of the things they took from you.

The tests were varied. Some were physical: endurance, pain threshold, reaction time, the body's response to chemical compounds that made your blood feel like it was boiling. Some were cognitive: pattern recognition, problem-solving, memory implantation and retrieval. Some were things you don't have words for, things that happened in the space between consciousness and unconsciousness where the mind bends in ways it was never designed to bend.

choices.text: You endured. You adapted. You waited for an opportunity.
You resisted from the beginning. They had to break you first.

human_041.title: Lab Escapee — Patient Endurance
text: You learned to be invisible. Not physically — they always knew where you were, the tracking implant in your spine saw to that — but psychologically. You gave them what they wanted: compliance, data, the appearance of a broken will. You performed on their tests like a machine, hitting the metrics they expected, never exceeding them by enough to attract special attention. Average. Manageable. Boring.

Meanwhile, behind the mask of compliance, you watched. You memorized guard rotations. You noted which doors required biometric access and which used keycards. You tracked the facility's rhythms — shift changes, supply deliveries, the brief window each evening when the night staff overlapped with the day shift and attention was divided. You built a map of the facility in your mind, corridor by corridor, room by room, exit by exit.

It took fourteen months. Fourteen months of needles and monitors and the voice saying 'Good morning, Subject 17' every day like a prayer from a god you didn't believe in. But when the opportunity came, you were ready.

choices.text: The opportunity was a power failure. You moved in the dark.
You turned a guard. Found the one with a conscience.
Another subject made the first move. You followed.

human_042.title: Lab Escapee — Resistance
text: You fought them from the first day you could remember. Not with fists — they had restraints for that, chemical sedatives that turned your muscles to water. You fought with refusal. You wouldn't eat. You wouldn't respond to commands. You wouldn't give them the data they wanted. You became the problem subject, the one the technicians dreaded, the one who required two guards for every procedure and tripled the time allocation on every test.

They escalated. Of course they did. First the sedatives, then the isolation, then the procedures that weren't tests at all but punishments disguised in clinical language. 'Aversion conditioning,' they called it. 'Behavioral modification.' Words that meant pain applied systematically until compliance was extracted like a tooth.

They broke you. You need to be honest about that. There was a point — you don't remember when, the memory is mercifully blurred — where the resistance stopped and the compliance began, not because you chose it but because the part of you capable of choosing had been worn smooth like a stone in a river. You did what they told you. You stopped screaming. You became quiet.

But quiet is not the same as empty. Deep inside the compliance, in a place they couldn't reach with their needles and their protocols, something survived. Something that watched and waited and remembered.

choices.text: The escape happened suddenly. Violently.
[Strength 11] Fight your way out. No more waiting.

human_043.title: Lab Escapee — Blackout
text: The power failed at 0247. You know the exact time because you'd been counting the seconds between the generator's cycles for months, and when the rhythm broke — when that steady mechanical heartbeat stuttered and died — the darkness that followed was the most beautiful thing you'd ever experienced.

Emergency lighting kicked in after twelve seconds. Red, dim, barely enough to navigate by. But you'd memorized the route in the dark. You'd practiced it in your mind a thousand times, running the path with your eyes closed until it was muscle memory. Cell door to corridor. Corridor to stairwell. Stairwell to service tunnel. Service tunnel to the world.

The electromagnetic locks on the cell doors defaulted to open during power failure — a safety feature designed to prevent subjects from being trapped during emergencies. The irony: the system designed to protect you was the system that set you free.

You ran. Barefoot on cold metal, wearing nothing but the facility's standard-issue grey jumpsuit, your heart beating so hard you could feel it in your teeth. Behind you, alarms began to sound. Ahead of you, in the dark, something that might have been freedom waited with open arms and sharp edges.

choices.text: You made it to the service tunnels. But you weren't alone.
[Dexterity 11] Move through the tunnels silently.

human_044.title: Lab Escapee — The Guard
text: His name was Ohen. Night shift, Block C, the quiet one who never hit the subjects and sometimes left an extra ration bar on your tray. You noticed him the way a drowning person notices a piece of driftwood — not as salvation, but as a possibility too fragile to trust.

You started small. Eye contact. A nod. The ghost of a smile when he checked your restraints and deliberately left them one notch looser than protocol required. Over weeks, the unspoken conversation between you grew into something more dangerous: words.

'Why are you here?' you asked one night, your voice rough from disuse. He didn't answer for a long time. Then: 'I needed the money. My daughter is sick. The treatment...' He trailed off. You understood. Everyone in the Lower District understands the arithmetic of desperation.

It took two months to convince him. Two months of careful persuasion, of appealing to the part of him that still recognized what he was doing as wrong. When he finally agreed to help you escape, his hands were shaking and his eyes were wet and you felt something you hadn't felt in a long time: guilt for what you were asking him to risk.

choices.text: Ohen opened the doors. You ran. You never learned what happened to him.
Don't look back. Never look back.

human_045.title: Lab Escapee — Subject 11
text: Subject 11 was in the cell next to yours. You'd heard her for months — the tapping on the wall, rhythmic, patterned. It took you weeks to realize it was code. Not any standard code, but something improvised, a system built from the ground up by a mind that refused to stop thinking even when thinking was punished.

Her name, she tapped, was Ren. She'd been in the facility longer than you. She'd figured out the guard rotations, the security blind spots, the weakness in the ventilation system's filtering mechanism. She had a plan. What she didn't have was someone to execute it with.

The plan was elegant and terrible: trigger an allergic reaction severe enough to require a medical transfer to the infirmary, then use the infirmary's external access port — the one used for emergency evacuations — to reach the service tunnels. The allergic reaction would be real. The risk was real. She might die in the attempt.

She went first. You heard the alarms, the running feet, the gurney wheeling past your cell. Twenty minutes later, the tap came through the wall again, weaker this time: 'I'm through. Your turn. Vent panel. Third screw is loose.'

choices.text: You followed her through. Together you ran.
Together is better. Even in the dark.

human_046.title: Lab Escapee — The Violent Break
text: It happened during a transfer between test chambers. Two guards, one on each arm, walking you down a corridor you'd walked a thousand times. The routine was so familiar it had become invisible, which is exactly what made it vulnerable. The guard on your left — the new one, the one who hadn't learned to keep his grip tight enough — sneezed. His hand loosened for half a second.
Half a second was enough.

The thing inside you — the part that had been watching and waiting behind the wall of compliance — moved. Not you, not exactly. Something older, something the facility's procedures had stripped down to pure survival instinct. You broke the left guard's grip, took his shock-baton, and hit him across the temple hard enough to feel the bone give. The right guard reached for his sidearm. You were faster. You'd always been faster. They'd tested you enough times to know that.

You don't remember the next four minutes clearly. They come back in flashes: a hallway, an alarm, blood on your hands that wasn't yours, a door that wouldn't open until you smashed the control panel and it defaulted to release. The sound of your own breathing, ragged and animal, echoing off concrete walls that had held you for years.

choices.text: You found the service tunnels. You ran until your legs gave out.
[Dexterity 10] Find the tunnels. Disappear.

human_047.title: Lab Escapee — The Tunnels
text: The service tunnels were a labyrinth. Miles of concrete and cable and darkness, stretching beneath the city like the roots of a dead tree. You ran through them in the grey jumpsuit with bare feet and no sense of direction, driven forward by the knowledge that stopping meant recapture and recapture meant a room with no windows and a voice that said 'Good morning, Subject 17' every day until you stopped being a person.

Time lost meaning. You might have run for hours or days. The tunnels branched and merged and looped, and you chose directions by instinct or desperation or the simple animal need to keep moving. You ate nothing. Drank water from condensation on the pipes. Slept in twenty-minute intervals, your back against a wall, one eye always open.

Eventually, the tunnels widened. The air changed — became thicker, heavier, tainted with the chemical signature of the Lower District's atmosphere. And then: light. Not the cold fluorescent light of the facility, but the dirty, orange, beautiful light of a street lamp filtering through a grate above your head.

choices.text: You emerged into the Lower District. A stranger in a strange world.
[Constitution 10] Keep running. Your legs are screaming but you're alive.

human_048.title: Lab Escapee — Surface World
text: The street hit you like a wall of sensation. Noise — horns, voices, the grinding of machines, the distant thunder of transport ships. Smell — food and filth and rain and the metallic undertaste of polluted air. Color — neon signs bleeding red and blue and green into the wet darkness, advertisements flickering on every surface, the dull orange of sodium lamps reflecting off puddles. After years of white walls and fluorescent lights, the sensory overload nearly brought you to your knees.

You stood on a street corner in a grey jumpsuit with no shoes and no identification and no memory of who you were before the facility, and the crowd flowed around you like water around a stone. Nobody stopped. Nobody asked if you were okay. In the Lower District, a bewildered person in institutional clothing is not unusual enough to warrant curiosity.

A vendor was selling synth-noodles from a cart across the street. The smell made your stomach clench so hard you bent double. You hadn't eaten real food — even synth-real — in months. Maybe years. The vendor saw you staring and made a gesture that was universally understood: money first.
You had no money. You had nothing except a grey jumpsuit, a tracking implant in your spine that might still be broadcasting, and a skull full of fragmented memories that didn't belong to you.

choices.text: You stole food. Old habits from a life you can't remember.
Someone approached you. They recognized the jumpsuit.

human_049.title: Lab Escapee — Survival Instinct
text: Your hands knew what to do even if your conscious mind didn't. The vendor turned to serve a customer and your fingers closed around a container of noodles with a precision that felt practiced, inherited from a life the facility had erased. You ate in an alley, crouched behind a dumpster, shoveling food into your mouth with your hands because you'd forgotten the concept of utensils. The noodles were synthetic and oversalted and the most extraordinary thing you'd ever tasted.

After eating, clarity returned in fragments. You assessed your situation with the methodical detachment the facility had trained into you: no identification, no resources, no allies, no knowledge of the city's geography or social structures. Active threats: the facility would be looking for you. The tracking implant was a beacon. You needed to find someone who could remove it — someone with medical skills and a willingness to help a person who didn't officially exist.

You found clothes first. A laundry line behind a residential block, drying in the acid rain. You traded the grey jumpsuit for a pair of stained work pants and an oversized jacket that smelled like engine oil. It wasn't much, but it was camouflage. You looked like everyone else now — tired, poor, invisible.

choices.text: You found a black-market surgeon in Sector 5. The implant had to come out.
[Intelligence 10] Research the surgeon thoroughly before trusting them.

human_050.title: Lab Escapee — Recognition
text: The woman who approached you was old — genuinely old, not the premature aging of the Lower District but the earned weariness of someone who'd outlasted expectations. She walked with a cane and wore a coat patched so many times it was more repair than original fabric. But her eyes were sharp, and they locked onto your grey jumpsuit with an intensity that made your skin crawl.

'Helion BioLab,' she said quietly, reading something in the jumpsuit's stitching that you couldn't see. 'Subject identifier. They've been running that facility for fifteen years. I was a nurse there when it opened.' She looked at your face — really looked, not the sliding glance of a stranger. 'How did you get out?'

You didn't answer. You couldn't. The words were trapped behind a wall of caution and confusion and the bone-deep fear that this was a test, that the facility had anticipated your escape and placed this woman in your path to lead you back.
'My name is Marta,' she said. 'And you need help. The implant in your spine — yes, I know about the implants — is broadcasting your location right now. We need to move.'
She extended her hand. Her fingers were steady, her gaze unwavering.

choices.text: Take her hand. You had no other options.
Run. Trust no one. Find your own way.

human_051.title: Lab Escapee — The Surgeon
text: The surgeon's name was Ink. At least, that's what the sign said — INK'S MODIFICATIONS AND REMOVALS, spray-painted on a metal door in a sub-basement beneath a noodle shop in Sector 5. The 'office' was a converted storage room with a surgical table, a rack of instruments that looked like they belonged in a mechanic's workshop, and a medical drone so old its hover field sputtered and coughed.

Ink herself was a thin woman with chrome hands and eyes that had been replaced with multi-spectrum scanners. She looked at you the way a butcher looks at a carcass — clinically, appraisingly, without sentiment.

'Spinal implant. Helion design. Broadcasting on three frequencies.' She scanned your back with a handheld device that beeped in a pattern you found deeply unsettling. 'I can remove it. It'll hurt. A lot. And it'll cost.' She named a number that might as well have been the GDP of a small nation for all you could pay it.
'I don't have credits,' you said. She looked at you for a long time. Then: 'Nobody who comes to me does. We'll work something out. You'll owe me. I always collect.'

choices.text: You let her cut. The pain was extraordinary but the freedom was worth it.
The pain is temporary. The freedom is permanent.

human_052.title: Lab Escapee — Marta's Help
text: Marta moved fast for someone with a cane. She led you through a maze of alleys and service corridors that she navigated from memory, never once looking at a map or hesitating at a junction. The Lower District was her territory, mapped in her mind with the precision of someone who'd been hiding in it for years.

Her safe house was a converted boiler room beneath an abandoned laundry in Sector 6. Clean, sparse, organized with military efficiency. Medical supplies lined one wall. A cot and a portable heater occupied the center. On a workbench, a collection of surgical tools gleamed under a bright lamp.

'I left Helion BioLab eleven years ago,' she said, unpacking a medical kit with practiced hands. 'What they were doing to those subjects — to people like you — I couldn't be part of it. But I couldn't stop it either. So I left, and I've been helping the ones who get out ever since.' She paused. 'There haven't been many.'

She removed the tracking implant that night. Local anesthetic, a scalpel, and steady hands that had done this before. The pain was manageable. The relief, when she held up the tiny device — no larger than a grain of rice, still blinking its location signal — was indescribable.

choices.text: Marta gave you a new identity. A chance to start over.
[Charisma 10] Thank Marta. She didn't have to help.

human_053.title: Lab Escapee — The Cutting
text: Ink didn't believe in general anesthesia. 'Too expensive, too risky, and unconscious patients can't tell me if I'm cutting something important.' So you lay face-down on the surgical table, biting a leather strap, and felt every second of the procedure. The incision. The probing. The metallic scrape of instruments against vertebrae. The moment when the implant came free and the electromagnetic hum you'd carried in your spine for years suddenly stopped, and the silence was so profound it made you weep.

Afterward, Ink stitched you up with the efficiency of someone who'd done this a hundred times and handed you a pill that dulled the pain to a bearable roar. 'You owe me,' she said again. 'And I'll be calling that debt in. Count on it.'

She let you sleep on the surgical table. It was the first night in years you slept without the implant's constant low-frequency buzz, and you dreamed — vivid, chaotic dreams that might have been memories: a house, a garden, a face you couldn't quite resolve. Someone who loved you, once. Someone you'd forgotten.
You woke up free. Free and indebted and utterly alone in a city that didn't know your name.

choices.text: You had to build a life from nothing. Again.
[Wisdom 10] Build carefully this time. Learn from the past.

human_054.title: Lab Escapee — New Identity
text: The identity was built from stolen data and forged documents: a name, a birth date, a citizen registration number belonging to someone who'd died three years ago and whose death hadn't been properly processed in the system. Marta assembled it with the care of an artist, layering false records and fabricated history until the digital ghost of a dead stranger became your new skin.

'It won't hold up to deep scrutiny,' Marta warned. 'Corporate background checks, military databases, anything above mid-level clearance will flag the inconsistencies. But for the Lower District? For getting a room, buying food, staying off the automated watch lists? It's enough.'

She gave you a name. Your name now. It felt foreign in your mouth, like wearing someone else's clothes. But you practiced saying it until it stopped catching in your throat, until it became something you could answer to without hesitation. A new name for a new life built on the ruins of one you couldn't remember.

Marta also gave you something else: a contact. Someone in Sector 7 who could get you work. 'Nothing glamorous,' she said. 'But honest enough, by Lower District standards. Stay quiet, stay small, and they won't find you.'

choices.text: You followed her advice. You tried to disappear into the Lower District.
Follow the advice. Blend in. Survive.

human_055.title: Lab Escapee — Building from Nothing
text: The first months were the hardest. Not physically — you'd endured worse in the facility — but psychologically. The world outside the lab was vast and chaotic and operated on rules nobody explained to you. Social interactions required a fluency you'd lost. Simple things — ordering food, navigating public transport, understanding the unspoken hierarchies of the street — became exhausting puzzles that left you drained and disoriented.

You took work where you could find it. Loading cargo at the transit hub. Cleaning industrial equipment at the Foundry. Jobs that required a strong back and no questions. The pay was meager, but it accumulated, and after six weeks you had enough for a room — a real room, with a door that locked and a window that looked out on something other than white walls.

At night, the memories came. Not your real memories — those were still locked behind whatever the facility had done to them — but the implanted ones, the residue of their tests. False memories of places you'd never been and people you'd never known, layered over the blank spaces where your actual past should have been. You couldn't tell the difference between what was real and what was fabricated, and eventually you stopped trying.

choices.text: You saw something that triggered a real memory. A fragment of your past.
You focused on survival. The past could wait.

human_056.title: Lab Escapee — The Fragment
text: A face in the market crowd. A woman with dark hair and a birthmark on her left cheek, carrying a bag of synth-produce. She passed within three feet of you, close enough to touch, and the sight of her hit you like an electrical surge — a full-body jolt of recognition so intense your vision whited out.

Not her, specifically. The memory she triggered. A kitchen. Sunlight through a window. The smell of real coffee — not synth, real — and a woman's voice saying your name. Not Subject 17. Your name. The one from before. It was right there, on the tip of your consciousness, a word you'd said a thousand times, and then it was gone, snatched back into the static like a signal interrupted.
You stood in the market with tears running down your face and no idea why. The crowd flowed around you. Nobody stopped.

choices.text: You tried to find the woman. She might know who you were.
You let it go. Some doors are better left closed.

human_057.title: Lab Escapee — The Search
text: You searched for three days. The market district was a maze of vendors and stalls and faces that blurred together in the neon light, and the woman with the birthmark was a ghost you'd conjured from a damaged memory. You asked vendors if they'd seen her. You described her features to strangers who looked at you with the pitying wariness reserved for the obviously unwell.

On the third day, a noodle vendor said he knew her. Regular customer. Name was Suki. Lived in the residential block on Ninth and Meridian. But when you went to the address, the building had been condemned. Yellow tape across the doors. A demolition notice dated three months ago.

You stood on the sidewalk and stared at the empty building and understood that whatever connection had existed between you and that face, it was gone. The facility hadn't just taken your memories — it had taken the time in which those memories had meaning. The people you'd known had moved on, aged, died, disappeared. You were a ghost returning to a graveyard.
The fragment of memory faded back into static, and you let it go.

choices.text: You accepted that the past was gone. The present was all you had.
The past is a ghost. Let it haunt someone else.

human_058.title: Lab Escapee — Convergence Point
text: You are a person without a past. The facility took that from you and gave you nothing in return except a body full of chemical markers and a mind that flinches at white walls. You've built a life — if you can call it that — from the scraps the Lower District offers: a job, a room, a daily routine that substitutes for purpose. But beneath the routine, beneath the careful normalcy you've constructed, there is a void where a history should be, and in that void something watches you with eyes that aren't your own.

Helion BioLab is still out there. The facility is still running. Other subjects are still waking to that voice — 'Good morning, Subject 17' — and you carry the knowledge of what happens behind those white walls like a stone in your chest. You could do something about it. Or you could disappear into the anonymity of the Lower District and hope they never find you again.
For now, there's a room in Sector 7. A place to be still. A place to decide.

choices.text: Go to the room. Close the door. Begin again.
You are a ghost. Embrace it.

human_059.title: Lab Escapee — Ink's Debt
text: Ink called in her debt six months after the surgery. A knock on your door at 2 AM, and there she was, chrome hands clasped behind her back, scanner eyes gleaming in the hallway's dim light. 'I need something delivered,' she said. 'No questions. No inspections. Sector 9 to the Neon Strip, by dawn.' She held out a sealed metal case the size of a shoebox.

You didn't ask what was inside. You'd agreed to the terms. But the weight of the case and the way Ink's eyes tracked it — with a care she never showed for people — told you it was valuable. Dangerous. Both.

The delivery went clean. You moved through the pre-dawn streets with the case under your arm, navigating by memory and instinct, arriving at the drop point with an hour to spare. The recipient was a man with no face — cosmetic surgery that had smoothed his features into a blank mask. He took the case without a word and disappeared into the Neon Strip's labyrinth of clubs and pleasure houses.
Ink considered the debt paid. But the faceless man remembered you. And he had his own jobs, his own needs, his own debts to call in.

choices.text: The work dried up eventually. You ended up where everyone ends up.
[Luck 10] Maybe something will turn up. It usually does.

human_060.title: Debt Slave — The Contract
text: The debt wasn't yours. That's the part that matters, the part you need people to understand even though nobody ever asks. It was your mother's debt — a medical bill for a procedure that kept her alive for six more months before the cancer ate through the treatment like acid through paper. The procedure cost 42,000 credits. Your mother died owing 38,000. And because she listed you as next of kin, because the fine print in the treatment contract included a hereditary liability clause that neither of you read because you were too busy being terrified that she was dying, the debt transferred to you on the morning of her funeral.

You were nineteen. You worked at a recycling plant in Sector 8, sorting components from dead electronics for minimum wage. The debt collectors came on a Tuesday — two men in grey suits with the Helion Financial Services logo on their lapels and smiles that were technically pleasant and functionally threatening. They explained your options with the patient condescension of people who've had this conversation a thousand times.

'Option one: you pay the debt. At your current income, that's approximately sixty-three years of payments, assuming no interest accrual, which of course there will be. Option two: you declare insolvency and accept an indentured labor contract. Duration: five years. After which, the debt is cleared.'
Five years. You could survive five years.

choices.text: You signed the contract. What choice did you have?
You tried to run. Debt doesn't chase you if it can't find you.

human_061.title: Debt Slave — The First Day
text: They took your clothes and gave you a grey uniform with your debt number stenciled on the back: HL-7734. Not a name — a ledger entry. The orientation officer, a bored man with a clipboard and the dead eyes of someone who'd processed ten thousand souls through this exact procedure, read you the terms: you would work where assigned, eat when permitted, sleep in the designated quarters. Your wages — such as they were — would be applied to the debt balance. Personal communication was restricted. Leaving the work compound without authorization was a contract violation punishable by extended service.

The compound was in the industrial district — a fenced facility built around a manufacturing plant that produced circuit boards for corporate electronics. The work was twelve hours of standing at a conveyor belt, soldering connections with a hand-tool that vibrated until your fingers went numb. Three hundred connections per shift. The quota was non-negotiable. Miss it and the lost production was added to your debt as a 'performance penalty.'

Your bunkmate was a woman named Rho. She'd been there three years. Her original contract was four. She'd accumulated enough performance penalties to add a fifth. 'The system is designed so you never finish,' she told you on your first night, lying in the bunk above yours, her voice flat with the particular exhaustion of someone who'd stopped being angry about their cage. 'The debt grows faster than you can pay it. That's the point.'

choices.text: You focused on the work. Head down, hands moving, counting the days.
You started looking for a way out from day one.

human_062.title: Debt Slave — Running from Debt
text: You lasted four months on the run. Four months of moving between sectors, sleeping in different places every night, using cash only because digital transactions leave trails. You changed your appearance — cut your hair, altered your walk, wore clothes that didn't match your build. You became a shadow, a rumor, a person-shaped absence in the system's records.
