But the system is patient. The system has algorithms and databases and facial recognition nets that span the entire city. On a rainy night in the transit hub, buying a ticket to Sector 12 with the last of your cash, a scanner caught the biometric signature you couldn't change — your retinal pattern, as unique as a fingerprint and impossible to fake.

The debt collectors were waiting when you stepped off the transport. Same grey suits. Same technically pleasant smiles. Same patient condescension. 'Mr./Ms. HL-7734,' one of them said, reading from a tablet. 'Your outstanding balance has accrued four months of default interest. Your total debt is now 46,200 credits. Your revised indentured labor contract has been extended to seven years.'
Seven years. The running had cost you two years of your life. They put a tracking anklet on you this time.

choices.text: Seven years in the compound. You had no choice now.
[Intelligence 10] Seven years is long enough to find every weakness in the system.

human_063.title: Debt Slave — The Grind
text: Days blurred into weeks. Weeks into months. The routine was its own kind of anesthesia: wake, work, eat, sleep. The soldering iron became an extension of your hand. Your fingers developed calluses so thick they lost most of their sensation. Your back bent from twelve hours of standing. Your eyes strained from the close work. And every two weeks, the updated debt statement appeared on your bunk's terminal screen, the balance shrinking so slowly it might as well have been standing still.

Rho became something between a friend and a fellow prisoner. She taught you the compound's rhythms — which supervisors could be reasoned with, which ones to avoid, how to pace your work to meet the quota without burning out. She'd been an accountant before the debt, she said. Good with numbers. The numbers here were simple: the debt grew at 8% annually. The wage credit was 3% of production value. The math was designed to ensure the balance never reached zero.

There were others in the compound who'd figured this out. A man called Brass who'd been there nine years on what was supposed to be a four-year contract. A teenager named Joy — the irony lost on no one — who'd inherited her parents' debt at sixteen. They didn't talk about the future. The future was a concept that had no currency in a place where time itself was owned by someone else.

choices.text: Something happened in the compound that changed things. An incident.
You found a way to manipulate the debt system. Rho helped.

human_064.title: Debt Slave — Looking for the Exit
text: The compound's security was designed to prevent exactly what you were planning. Perimeter fence with vibration sensors. Guard towers with searchlights. Electronic monitoring of every worker's location via the ID chip embedded in your uniform. The gates opened twice a day — once for the morning shift, once for the supply trucks — and both openings were heavily monitored.

But you watched. You listened. You learned the system the way you'd learned everything else in your life: through patient, desperate observation. And after three months, you found the weakness: the drainage system. The compound was built on low ground, and during heavy rains, the water had to go somewhere. The drainage channels ran beneath the perimeter fence and connected to the city's municipal water system.

The channels were small. Barely wide enough for a human body. They were grated at the fence line, but the grates were old and the bolts corroded. You could work them loose over time, a quarter-turn per night, hidden under the noise of the rain.
The question was whether to go alone.

choices.text: Tell Rho. She deserved to know.
Tell no one. The more people who know, the higher the risk.

human_065.title: Debt Slave — The Incident
text: The fire started in Assembly Line 3. A faulty power coupling, they said afterward, though everyone knew the equipment hadn't been properly maintained in years because maintenance cost money and workers were cheaper to replace than machines. The flames spread fast — faster than anyone expected — and the emergency exits were locked because they were always locked because open exits were an escape risk.

Fourteen workers died. Fourteen people burned to death or suffocated on chemical smoke behind locked doors while the compound's security team debated whether to override the lockdown protocol. Rho wasn't among the dead. Neither were you. But Brass was. And Joy. And eleven others whose names you'd never learned because in the compound, names were a luxury that the debt didn't cover.

The aftermath was quiet. Helion Financial Services issued a statement expressing 'regret for the workplace incident' and announced that the deceased workers' outstanding debts would be 'forgiven posthumously.' Forgiven. As if the dead could appreciate the generosity.

Something crystallized in you during the memorial — a brief, perfunctory gathering in the compound yard where a supervisor read names from a list without once looking up. The system wasn't broken. It was working exactly as designed. And you refused to be ground to dust inside it.

choices.text: The fire created chaos. You used it to escape.
You organized. Quietly. The other workers were angry enough to listen.

human_066.title: Debt Slave — Cooking the Books
text: Rho had been an accountant. A good one. She understood the debt system not as an abstract injustice but as a mechanical process — inputs, outputs, calculations, variables. And like any mechanical process, it had vulnerabilities.

'The production tracking is automated,' she explained one night, her voice barely above a whisper. 'Each board you solder gets scanned and logged. The scan feeds directly into the wage credit system. But the scanner has a recalibration window — thirty seconds every four hours when it resets. During that window, it double-counts.'

If you timed your output to coincide with the recalibration windows — four extra boards per shift, double-counted — you could inflate your wage credits by roughly 15% without anyone noticing. It was small. Incremental. The kind of advantage that only mattered over months and years. But months and years were all you had.

It worked for eight months. Eight months of carefully timed production, of watching your debt balance shrink just a little faster than it should, of Rho monitoring the numbers with an accountant's eye for anomalies. Then the system updated.

choices.text: The audit caught the discrepancy. Penalties. Extended service.
You stopped before the audit caught you. But the margin you'd gained made a difference.

human_067.title: Debt Slave — Telling Rho
text: Rho listened to your plan in the dark of the bunk room, her face unreadable. When you finished, she was quiet for a long time. Then: 'The drainage channel. I've thought about it too. The grate bolts are standard M8 — I checked. Corroded enough to work loose by hand if you're patient. But the channel floods during heavy rain, and the weather system says we're due for a storm cycle in two weeks.'
She'd already been planning. Of course she had. Rho was an accountant — she didn't leave numbers unexamined, and escape was just another equation to solve.

'If we go, we go together,' she said. 'And we go during the storm. The rain will mask the noise, the flooding will wash away traces, and the guards won't patrol the drainage perimeter in heavy weather. Their contracts don't cover hazard conditions.'
The irony — escape enabled by the same contract culture that had imprisoned you — hung in the air between you like something you could almost touch.

choices.text: You waited for the storm. You escaped together.
[Dexterity 11] Move through the storm like a ghost.

human_068.title: Debt Slave — Going Alone
text: You worked the grate bolts at night, alone, your fingers raw and bleeding on the corroded metal. Quarter-turn by quarter-turn, night after night, while the compound slept around you and the rain drummed on the roof like impatient fingers. You didn't tell Rho. You didn't tell anyone. Trust was a luxury you'd never been able to afford, and the stakes were too high for sentimentality.

On the twenty-third night, the last bolt came free. The grate shifted in its frame with a metallic groan that sounded impossibly loud in the dark. You froze, waiting for the searchlight, the shout, the alarm. Nothing. The guards were in their tower, warm and dry and watching monitors that didn't cover the drainage perimeter.

You slid into the channel feet-first. The water was cold — shockingly, paralyzingly cold — and it rose to your chest immediately, surging with the force of the storm. For a terrible moment you couldn't breathe, couldn't think, couldn't do anything except cling to the channel walls and fight the current that wanted to drag you under.
But you'd survived worse. You'd always survived worse.

choices.text: You made it through the channel. You emerged free.
[Constitution 11] Push through the channel. Don't stop.

human_069.title: Debt Slave — Freedom
text: The first breath of free air tasted like chemicals and rain and garbage, and it was the sweetest thing you'd ever drawn into your lungs. You stood in an alley somewhere in the industrial district, soaking wet, shivering, the tracking anklet still on your wrist — you'd need to deal with that — and the compound behind you, a cluster of lights in the distance getting smaller with every step.

If Rho was with you, she stood beside you in the rain and laughed — a quiet, disbelieving sound, like someone who'd just heard a joke they'd given up hoping for. If you were alone, you stood in the silence and let the rain wash the compound's smell off your skin and listened to your own heartbeat and marveled at the simple, revolutionary fact that nobody was telling you what to do.

The tracking anklet had to go. A maintenance hatch on a nearby building yielded a length of wire and a piece of scrap metal. Fifteen minutes of work — twisting, prying, leveraging — and the anklet cracked open and fell to the ground. The GPS signal died. For the first time in years, nobody knew where you were.
You were free. Free and broke and hunted and standing in the rain in a city that didn't care whether you lived or died. But free.

choices.text: You needed to disappear. New identity, new life.
[Intelligence 10] Plan the disappearance carefully. No loose ends.

human_070.title: Debt Slave — The Organizer
text: You started with whispers. A word here, a glance there, careful conversations in the noise of the assembly line where the monitoring mics couldn't pick up individual voices. The fire had done what years of grinding servitude couldn't: it had made people angry enough to listen. Fourteen dead. Locked doors. A corporate statement that treated human lives like accounting errors. Even the most broken spirits had a threshold, and Helion had found it.

Rho was your first recruit. Then Brass's bunkmate, a quiet man named Sol who'd been a union organizer before the debt swallowed him. Sol knew how to build a network — small cells, limited information sharing, dead drops and codes. Within two months, you had thirty workers ready to act.

The plan wasn't violent. Sol was clear about that: 'Violence gives them the excuse they need to bring in Sec-Corp. We stop working. All of us. At the same time. The production line goes silent, and every day it stays silent costs them more than our combined debts. Economic pressure. It's the only language they speak.'
The strike lasted three days before Helion sent in the enforcers.

choices.text: The enforcers broke the strike. But the chaos gave you a chance to run.
The chaos was your chance. You took it.

human_071.title: Debt Slave — The Audit
text: The auditor was a thin man with augmented eyes that scrolled data across his retinas in visible green lines. He sat in the supervisor's office for two days, cross-referencing production logs with wage credit allocations, and on the third day he called you and Rho into the room with the expression of someone who'd found an interesting specimen under a microscope.

'Production anomaly,' he said, turning his tablet so you could see the graphs. 'Statistically significant deviation in output timing correlating with scanner recalibration windows. Either the machine is malfunctioning, or someone is gaming the system.' He looked at you. Looked at Rho. 'The machine was tested. It's fine.'

Rho took the blame. All of it. She told them you didn't know, that she'd manipulated your production schedule without your knowledge. She lied with the calm precision of an accountant presenting a balance sheet, and they believed her because she was the numbers person, the one with the skills to pull it off.
They added two years to Rho's contract. Performance penalties. You watched them lead her away and felt the weight of a debt that no ledger could calculate.

choices.text: You couldn't leave Rho behind. You had to get both of you out.
[Charisma 10] Convince Rho to trust you. Together is safer.

human_072.title: Debt Slave — Through the Channel
text: The drainage channel was two hundred meters of hell. Water surging against your body, the concrete walls pressing in from both sides, the absolute darkness broken only by the distant, diffused glow of the compound's lights behind you. Twice the current pulled you under, and twice you fought back to the surface, gasping, sputtering, your fingers scraping against the walls for purchase.

The channel narrowed at the perimeter fence crossing. For a terrifying moment you were stuck — your shoulders jammed between the concrete walls, the water rising, the current pulling — and then you twisted sideways, felt something in your shoulder pop, and squeezed through with a burst of pain that whited out your vision.

The channel emptied into a municipal storm drain that fed the Lower District's water recycling system. You crawled out of the drain pipe into a culvert, lay on the wet ground, and stared at the sky. The rain was still falling. The compound's lights were a faint glow behind you, already distant, already part of a past tense you were writing with every breath.
Your shoulder was dislocated. Your hands were bleeding. Your uniform was ruined. And you were free.

choices.text: You needed to disappear into the Lower District.
You're free. Now learn what that means.

human_073.title: Debt Slave — Convergence Point
text: The debt is paid. Not by credits — by blood and sweat and years you'll never get back. The compound is behind you, the contract voided by escape or endurance or sheer stubborn refusal to be consumed. You're free in the way that a released animal is free: wary, scarred, unaccustomed to open spaces, flinching at sounds that once meant punishment.

Helion Financial Services has marked you as a defaulted account. The debt still exists on paper, a number in a database that will follow you through every background check and credit inquiry for the rest of your life. But you're not in the compound anymore. You're not soldering circuit boards for someone else's profit. You're standing on a street in the Lower District with nothing to your name except the clothes on your back and the lessons written on your body.

There's a room in Sector 7. Cheap enough, anonymous enough, far enough from the industrial district that the compound feels like a bad dream. It's not much. But it's yours. The first thing that's been truly yours in years.

choices.text: Take the room. Start over.
The debt is paid. You are free.

human_080.title: The Fixer's Work
text: Greel's jobs escalated the way water heats — gradually enough that you didn't notice the boiling point until you were already in it. Deliveries became surveillance. Surveillance became sabotage. Sabotage became things that required you to shower for a long time afterward and avoid mirrors for a few days.

The Neon Strip became your office. That gaudy, electric artery of the Lower District where pleasure and danger held hands and danced, where the clubs never closed and the money was dirty and the lights were bright enough to blind you to what was happening in the shadows between them. Greel's clients were mid-level criminals, the kind who couldn't afford to hire professionals but could afford you — cheap, deniable, desperate enough to not ask questions.

You were good at it. That was the worst part. The skills the streets had taught you — silence, speed, the ability to read a room and find the danger — translated seamlessly into Greel's world. You made enough to eat. Enough for a room. Enough to feel like you were going somewhere, even if 'somewhere' was just deeper into a hole that would eventually collapse on top of you.

choices.text: Then Greel got killed, and his debts became your problem.
You realized you were becoming someone you didn't want to be.

human_081.title: The Neon Court Connection
text: The evidence was circumstantial at first. A logo on a shipping crate that shouldn't have been there. A meeting Greel took with someone wearing too-clean clothes for the Lower District. The way certain jobs required you to avoid specific streets at specific times, as if the patrol schedules were being coordinated with someone on the inside. Piece by piece, the picture assembled itself: Greel wasn't an independent operator. He was a subcontractor. And the entity holding his contract was the Neon Court — the same criminal syndicate that ran half the Lower District's economy through a combination of violence, corruption, and the bureaucratic precision of a legitimate corporation.
You confronted him in the Rust Bucket, after hours, when the bar was empty and the air smelled like stale liquor and regret. Greel didn't deny it. He didn't even look surprised.

'Everyone works for someone,' he said, pouring himself a drink. 'The question isn't who signs your checks. The question is whether you can live with what they ask you to do.' He looked at you with those calculating eyes. 'So far, you've been living with it just fine.'
He was right. And that was the problem.

choices.text: You kept working for Greel. The money was too good to walk away.
You walked away. Cut ties. Started over.

human_082.title: A Face from the Past
text: You saw Vasquez in a bar in Sector 7. She looked twenty years older than the last time you'd seen her, though only five had passed. The prosthetic jaw was the same, but everything else had eroded — the authority, the posture, the certainty in her eyes that had once made you follow her into burning buildings. She was drinking alone at a corner table, her coat stained, her hands wrapped around a glass like it was the only anchor keeping her from drifting away entirely.

She recognized you. Something flickered in her eyes — not quite surprise, more like the dull acknowledgment of an inevitability she'd been expecting. 'Sit,' she said. The same command voice. But emptied of everything that had once given it power.

She'd been discharged after the court-martial. Dishonorable. Same as you, maybe. The details differed but the outcome was the same: a career destroyed, a purpose dissolved, a person unmade. She'd drifted the same way you had, through the same Lower District gravity well, until she'd ended up here, in this bar, at this table, drinking away a pension that had been denied.
'I think about Ashfall every day,' she said. 'Every single day.'

choices.text: You stayed with her. Two broken soldiers in a broken world.
Seeing her was too painful. You left before the old wounds reopened.

human_083.title: Rock Bottom
text: Rock bottom, when you find it, isn't dramatic. It's not a crash or a collapse or a moment of shattering clarity. It's waking up on a heating grate at 4 AM because the grate cycled off and the cold woke you, and lying there on the metal lattice looking up at a sky you can't see through the city's canopy, and realizing that you can't remember the last time someone said your name.
Not your street name. Not your service number. Not your debt identifier. Your name. The one your mother gave you. The one that used to mean something to someone.

You lay there on the grate and felt the cold seep into your bones and thought about the trajectory of your life — the choices, the losses, the slow gravitational pull of a system designed to compress people like you into nothing — and you understood, with a clarity that was neither comforting nor cruel, that you had two options: stop here, or start again.
The grate cycled back on. Warmth seeped up through the metal. You lay there for another minute, feeling it, the simple animal comfort of heat against cold skin. Then you stood up.

choices.text: Start again. Find a room. Find a reason.
You survived. That is the only story that matters.

human_084.title: Lab Escapee — The Memory
text: A face in the market crowd. A woman with dark hair and a birthmark on her left cheek, carrying a bag of synth-produce. She passed within three feet of you, close enough to touch, and the sight of her hit you like an electrical surge — a full-body jolt of recognition so intense your vision whited out.

Not her, specifically. The memory she triggered. A kitchen. Sunlight through a window. The smell of real coffee — not synth, real — and a woman's voice saying your name. Not Subject 17. Your name. The one from before. It was right there, on the tip of your consciousness, and then it was gone.
You stood in the market with tears running down your face and no understanding of why.

choices.text: You tried to chase the memory. Follow the thread.
You let it go. The past is a country you can't return to.

human_085.title: Lab Escapee — Moving Forward
text: The past was a locked room. Maybe someday you'd find the key, but today wasn't that day, and you had more immediate concerns: food, shelter, the slow accumulation of resources required to maintain existence in a city that charged you for the privilege of breathing its poisoned air.

You took work at the transit hub. Loading and unloading cargo containers, twelve hours a day, cash payment, no questions. The work was physical and mindless and perfect for someone who needed to not think. Your body — whatever the facility had done to it — was stronger than it should have been, more resilient, quicker to recover. The other laborers noticed. They gave you a wide berth.

Months passed. You settled into a routine that wasn't quite life but was its closest available substitute. And in the quiet moments — the spaces between shifts, the minutes before sleep — you felt the facility's shadow lengthen behind you, a reminder that you hadn't escaped so much as been temporarily misplaced.

choices.text: The routine held. Until it didn't.
[Wisdom 10] The routine is a cage. See it for what it is.

human_086.title: Debt Slave — Disappearing
text: Disappearing in the Lower District is both easier and harder than it sounds. Easier because nobody looks. The Lower District is a sea of invisible people — unregistered, undocumented, uncounted. Harder because the systems that track you are automated, tireless, and indifferent. Cameras. Scanners. The vast digital net that catalogs every transaction, every transit pass, every biometric scan.

You learned to live off-grid. Cash only. No transit cards. No registered addresses. You changed your appearance — shaved your head, altered your gait, wore clothes that didn't match any profile in the corporate databases. You became a ghost, the kind of person that algorithms skip because the data points don't correlate into a recognizable pattern.

It cost you everything. Connections. Comfort. The basic human infrastructure of being known by someone, anyone. But you were free, and freedom, you discovered, has a taste: metallic, slightly bitter, cold on the tongue. Like the air in the Lower District. Like rain on the metal streets. Like the rest of your life stretching out ahead of you, unmapped and terrifying and yours.

choices.text: Eventually you found stability. A room. A baseline.
[Intelligence 10] Find stability on your own terms. Think it through.

human_087.title: Greel's End
text: They found Greel in the Rust Bucket on a Sunday morning, slumped behind the bar with three rounds in his chest and the till untouched. It wasn't a robbery. It was a message. The Neon Court doesn't kill its assets casually — when they do, it's a communique written in blood, addressed to everyone in the dead person's orbit. The message was clear: Greel had done something wrong. And everyone connected to him was now a loose end.

You heard about it from a vendor in the market district who'd heard it from a bartender who'd heard it from someone who'd seen the body. The Lower District's information network is faster than any corporate comm system. Within hours, you knew the details. Within a day, you knew the Neon Court was looking for Greel's associates.
Associates. That meant you.

You packed what you could carry — which wasn't much, it was never much — and moved to a new sector. Then moved again. Then again. The Court's reach was long but not infinite, and you'd spent your entire life being small and fast and forgettable. These skills, refined over years of survival, were the only things standing between you and a Sunday morning behind a bar.

choices.text: The running stopped when you ran out of places to run to.
The running stops when you decide it stops.

human_088.title: Starting Over
text: You've lost count of how many times you've started over. The number itself has become meaningless — what matters is the act, the decision to stand up one more time after being knocked down, to walk into the unknown with empty hands and a battered heart and the stubborn, irrational conviction that there's something on the other side of all this pain that's worth reaching.

The Lower District is full of people who've stopped reaching. You see them on every corner — the collapsed, the surrendered, the ones who've let the city's gravity flatten them into two-dimensional outlines of the people they used to be. You've been close to joining them. Closer than you'll ever admit. But something in you — call it instinct, call it stubbornness, call it the thing that makes a human being a human being instead of a mechanism — keeps pulling you back from the edge.

There's a room in Sector 7. A small room, a nothing room, but a room with a door that locks and a window that shows you a sliver of the sky. It costs almost everything you have. But it's a start. It's always a start.

choices.text: Take the room. Close the door. Breathe.
Close the door. Lock it. This is yours now.

human_089.title: Two Soldiers
text: You sat with Vasquez until the bar closed. Then you sat with her on the street. Two veterans of a war nobody declared, sharing a bottle of something that burned going down and sitting warm in the stomach like a small, temporary hearth.

She told you about the years since her discharge. The anger that had curdled into something quieter and more corrosive. The jobs she'd taken and lost. The nights that stretched too long and the mornings that came too fast. You recognized every word because they were your words too, different details but the same story, written in the same exhausted hand.

'I keep thinking I should feel guilty about Ashfall,' she said, the bottle balanced on her knee. 'And I do. But the guilt isn't the worst part. The worst part is that I'd do it differently if I could go back. I'd refuse sooner. I'd be braver. And it wouldn't have mattered. They would have done it anyway. We were never the point. We were just the hands they used.'
She left before dawn. Gave you an address — a boarding house in Sector 7 where the landlord was ex-military and didn't ask questions. 'Find a room,' she said. 'Then find a reason to keep it.'

choices.text: Follow Vasquez's advice. Get the room.
[Wisdom 10] Vasquez knows this city. Listen.

human_090.title: The Room
text: Sector 7, Block 14, Room 7C. That's the address. Your address. It sounds like a coordinate in a system designed to reduce human beings to grid references, and that's exactly what it is, but it's also the first fixed point in your life in longer than you can remember.

The room is small. Obscenely small. A cot against one wall, a metal shelf bolted to another, a window that looks out on the building across the alley — close enough to touch, if you leaned out, which you shouldn't because the window frame is rusted and might not hold your weight. The ceiling has water stains that map a geography of previous leaks. The walls are thin enough to hear your neighbors arguing, or crying, or doing both at the same time.

You sit on the cot and feel the springs protest under your weight and look at the room — your room, your space, your four walls and a door — and something happens that you don't expect. You cry. Not from sadness, though there's plenty of that. Not from relief, though there's some of that too. You cry because the room is real and you're real and the distance between where you started and where you are is a journey that should have killed you, and it didn't, and you don't know what to do with the weight of your own survival.

choices.text: Look out the window at the city that tried to break you.
Lie down. Rest. For the first time in a long time, rest.
Look in the cracked mirror. See who you have become.

human_091.title: The View
text: Through the dirty glass, the Lower District sprawls beneath you like a wound. Buildings stacked on buildings, connected by bridges and catwalks and improvised structures that defy both engineering and gravity. Neon signs flicker against the eternal rain, advertising products you can't afford and services you don't need. Patrol drones move through the air in lazy patterns, their searchlights painting white circles on the wet streets below.

Above it all — so far above that it might as well be another planet — the Cloud Towers glow against the dark sky. The upper city, where the air is clean and the food is real and death is optional for anyone who can afford a memory stack. You've never been there. You'll probably never go. The elevator that connects the districts runs on credits and clearance levels, and you have neither.
But you're alive. Down here, in the filth and the noise and the chemical rain, you're alive. And that counts for something. It has to count for something, because it's all you've got.

Tomorrow you'll need to find work. Food. A way to keep this room and this fragile, improbable life you've assembled from the wreckage. But tonight, you can stand at this window and watch the city that tried to grind you to nothing, and you can tell it — silently, fiercely, with everything you have left — that you're still here.

choices.text: You notice something on the shelf. A message, left by the previous tenant.
Turn away from the window. There's nothing out there but rain and neon.

human_092.title: Rest
text: You lie down on the cot. The springs groan. The mattress is thin enough that you can feel the metal frame through it. But it's a bed — a real bed, in a room with a locked door — and the simple security of that fact settles over you like a physical weight, pressing the tension out of muscles that have been coiled for years.

Sleep comes slowly, the way it always does when you're not sure you'll be safe through the night. Your body goes through its checks — listening for footsteps, monitoring the door, cataloging the sounds of the building — before gradually, reluctantly, releasing its grip on consciousness.

You dream. Not the facility's implanted dreams, not the nightmares of fire and smoke and friends dying. Something simpler. A hand on your shoulder. A voice that says your name with warmth instead of designation. A feeling — distant, faded, but unmistakable — of being known by someone. Of mattering.

You wake up four hours later to rain on the window and the grey light of a Lower District morning. The dream is already fading, but the feeling lingers, a warm residue in your chest that you hold onto like a coal in cupped hands.

choices.text: Get up. Look around the room properly. Take stock of what you have.
[Intelligence 10] Inventory everything. Knowledge is survival.

human_093.title: What's Left Behind
text: On the metal shelf, wedged between the wall and the bracket, someone left a note. A scrap of synth-paper with words written in a hand that shook — from cold or fear or exhaustion, you can't tell. The ink has bled and faded, but you can make out most of it:

'If you're reading this, you got the room. Good for you. I had it before you. Couldn't keep it. Couldn't keep much of anything in the end. But listen — it's not the room that matters. It's the door. A door you can close. A line between you and everything out there that wants to eat you alive. Guard the door. Guard whatever you've got left inside you that's still worth guarding. This city will take everything if you let it. Don't let it.'
No name. No date. Just the words of someone who was here before you, who fought the same fight, who left behind the only weapon they had: the knowledge that survival is an act of defiance.

You fold the note carefully and put it in your pocket. Then you look at the room — at the cot, the shelf, the window, the door — and you feel something settle into place inside you. Not hope. Something harder than hope and more durable. Resolve.

choices.text: Guard the door. Guard what's left. Begin.
Guard what you have. Build from here.

human_094.title: The First Morning
text: Morning in the Lower District arrives as a gradual lightening of the gloom rather than any actual sunrise. The city's upper levels block the direct sun; what filters down is grey and diffuse, the light of a world experienced secondhand. Rain is still falling — it's almost always falling — and through your window you can see the day shift beginning: workers emerging from their blocks like ants from disturbed earth, heads down, moving with the mechanical purpose of people who've learned to conserve every joule of energy for the work ahead.

Your stomach growls. There's a noodle vendor three blocks east who sells synth-ramen for two credits a bowl. A job board at the transit hub posts day-labor opportunities. The free clinic in Sector 3 opens at 0700 — you should get checked, make sure whatever the streets or the compound or the facility left in your system isn't going to kill you on a delay.

But first: the mirror. There's a small, cracked mirror on the wall — standard issue in Lower District housing, as if someone in city planning thought that self-reflection was a basic amenity. You stand in front of it and look at yourself. The face that looks back is tired, scarred, older than its years. But the eyes are alive. Whatever the world has taken from you, it hasn't taken that.

choices.text: Step outside. Face the city. Whatever comes next, you'll meet it standing.
[Charisma 10] Meet the city with confidence. Fake it if you must.

human_095.title: The Street
text: The hallway outside your room smells like mildew and cooking oil. Three other doors, all closed. From behind one, the sound of a child laughing — incongruous, almost alien in this place, but real. From another, the blue flicker of a terminal screen. The third is silent in the way that suggests either absence or a stillness too practiced to be natural.

The stairs down are narrow and steep, the treads worn smooth by thousands of feet over decades of use. The building's lobby — a generous term for a concrete box with a mailbox bank and a broken elevator — opens onto a street that hits you with the full sensory assault of the Lower District at rush hour.

Rain. Always rain. The drops are faintly acidic, and the locals wear hoods and treated coats to keep the worst of it off their skin. You'll need to acquire one. You'll need to acquire a lot of things. But acquisition requires credits, and credits require work, and work requires finding someone willing to hire a person with no references, no history, and a face that says 'I've seen things' in a district where everyone has seen things.
The day begins. Your new life — whatever it will be — starts now, with this step, on this street, in this rain.

choices.text: Head to the job board at the transit hub.
Find food first. You can't think on an empty stomach.

human_096.title: The Job Board
text: The transit hub's job board is a wall-mounted terminal with a cracked screen and a queue of people waiting to swipe through listings that update every thirty minutes. The jobs are what you'd expect: manual labor, security details, medical testing for corp pharma trials that pay well because the risks are things they don't want to explain in writing. The better jobs — the ones that require skills or clearances — flash briefly before being claimed by people with faster connections and stronger references.

You stand in the queue and wait your turn. The man ahead of you is an orc with industrial burns covering half his face. He scrolls through the listings with a finger thick as a pipe wrench, muttering under his breath. The woman behind you is a synth with a cracked faceplate, her optical sensors flickering irregularly. Everyone here is damaged in some visible way. Everyone here is looking for the same thing: enough credits to survive another day.
When your turn comes, you find a listing that doesn't make you flinch: WAREHOUSE LABOR, SECTOR 7 DEPOT. 12HR SHIFTS. 15 CREDITS/DAY. NO BACKGROUND CHECK. It's not much. It's enough.

choices.text: Take the warehouse job. Start building.
Keep looking. There might be something better.

human_097.title: The Noodle Stand
text: The noodle vendor is an old man with cybernetic arms that move with the fluid precision of someone who's been making the same food for forty years. His cart is a marvel of compressed engineering: burner, wok, prep surface, and ingredient storage all packed into a space no larger than a coffin. The noodles are synth-wheat in a broth made from reconstituted protein paste and industrial-grade flavor compounds. They taste like salt and warmth and the particular mercy of having something hot in your stomach when the world is cold.

You eat standing up, hunched over the bowl to keep the rain out, and watch the morning traffic flow past. This is your neighborhood now. These are your streets. The vendor, the buildings, the people — the woman selling bootleg tech from a blanket, the kids running through the crowd, the patrol drone hovering at the intersection like a mechanical angel with bad intentions — all of it is the landscape of your new existence.
The noodles cost two credits. You have thirteen left. At this rate, you can eat for six more days. On day seven, the math runs out.
Better find work.

choices.text: Head to the job board. Time is money. Money is survival.
[Intelligence 10] Study the job board. Find the best opportunity.

human_098.title: Day One
text: The warehouse is a cavernous space in the industrial sector where shipping containers are stacked three high and the air smells like metal and hydraulic fluid. Your job is simple: move crates from the intake dock to the sorting area, scan them, and stack them in the designated zones. The crates are heavy. The pace is relentless. The supervisor is a drone — literally, an AI-controlled hovering unit that monitors productivity and issues corrections in a flat, genderless voice.

You work alongside a dozen others. Nobody talks much. The drone supervisor discourages 'non-essential vocalization.' But in the brief breaks — ten minutes every four hours — conversations happen in compressed, efficient bursts. Names exchanged. Neighborhoods compared. The universal Lower District small talk of survival: where to eat cheap, which clinics don't require ID, which streets to avoid after dark.

At the end of twelve hours, the drone credits your account with fifteen credits. Fifteen credits. A number so small it's almost abstract, but it represents something concrete: you worked today. You earned today. You existed in the world as a person who does things and receives compensation, however meager, for doing them.
You walk home in the rain. Your body aches. Your hands are raw. And in the center of your chest, in the space where purpose used to be, something small and fragile begins to take root.

choices.text: Tomorrow you'll do it again. And the day after. This is your life now. But it's yours.
Today was hard. Tomorrow will be harder. But you'll be ready.

human_099.title: The Threshold
text: You stand at the door of Room 7C. Your room. Your door. The key card is warm in your hand from being held too tightly, and the lock beeps green when you swipe it, and the door opens onto the small, shabby, imperfect space that is yours and nobody else's.

Behind you, the hallway. The building. The street. The district. The city. The entire towering, grinding, indifferent machine of civilization that has spent your whole life trying to reduce you to a statistic, a number, a body in a grey uniform. It's all still there. It will always be there.

But here, on this side of the door, there is a cot and a shelf and a window and a cracked mirror and a note from a stranger who understood, and these things are enough. They have to be enough. Because this is where it starts — not the story you've been telling yourself, the one about loss and damage and survival, but a new one. A story that begins with a door closing and a person standing alone in a small room and choosing, deliberately and with full knowledge of the cost, to keep going.
You close the door. You lock it. You stand in the silence of your own space and breathe.

choices.text: Sleep. Tomorrow, everything changes.
[Wisdom 10] Sleep deep. Tomorrow everything changes.

human_100.title: Origin Complete
text: You sleep deeply for the first time in memory. No dreams. No nightmares. Just the dark, warm absence of consciousness that your body has been craving for years, held in the small safety of a locked room in a forgotten corner of a city that doesn't know your name.

When you wake, the rain has stopped. Through the window, a sliver of grey light falls across the cot. The building is quiet. The street below is quiet. For one suspended moment, the entire Lower District holds its breath.

Then the city starts again — the hum of generators, the distant roar of transport ships, the voices and footsteps and mechanical rhythms of a million people beginning another day of survival. And you, in your room, on your cot, with your raw hands and your aching body and your small, fierce, stubborn heart, begin with them.

Whatever you were before — street rat, soldier, lab subject, debt slave — you are something else now. Something the city hasn't categorized yet. Something that doesn't fit neatly into its systems of control and exploitation. You are a person at the beginning of a story, and the story is yours to write.
The door is waiting. The city is waiting. And somewhere out there, in the neon-stained dark, your future is taking shape — dangerous, uncertain, and unmistakably alive.

choices.text: Open the door. Step into the light.
The light is blinding after so long in shadow. Step into it anyway.

human_074.title: Street Rat Ending: The Survivor
text: You have been surviving since before you could walk. The streets raised you, broke you, taught you everything you know about the ugly mathematics of staying alive. Every scar is a lesson. Every lost friend is a diploma. You graduated from the school of nothing with honors, and your reward is this: a room, a heartbeat, and the knowledge that you are harder to kill than anything the Lower District can throw at you.

The street rat does not dream of better things. The street rat dreams of making it through the night, and then doing it again the next night, and the next, until the nights stack up into something that might, from a distance, resemble a life.

You close the door of Room 7C and lock it and stand in the silence and feel, for the first time in memory, the absence of threat. No one is chasing you. No one is hunting you. You are invisible, and for a street rat, invisibility is the closest thing to safety this world offers.

Tomorrow, you will need to eat, to work, to navigate the endless maze of survival. But tonight, behind a locked door, in a room that is yours, you can rest. You were born ready. The streets made sure of that.

choices.text: Open the door. Step into the light.
The street rat survives. It's what you do.

human_075.title: Ex-Soldier Ending: The Haunted
text: The uniform is gone but the soldier remains. You carry the military in your posture, in your reflexes, in the way your eyes scan every room for exits and your body tenses at sudden sounds. The training is permanent. The memories are permanent.

Room 7C is not a barracks. The cot is not a military bunk. But your body does not know the difference. You lie down with your boots on and your hands at your sides and stare at the ceiling and count the water stains the way you used to count stars through the barracks window.

You are a soldier without a war. But the Lower District is its own kind of battlefield. And soldiers, even broken ones, even haunted ones, are built to fight. Whatever comes next — whatever enemy, whatever mission, whatever cause worth bleeding for — you will meet it the way you have met everything: on your feet, eyes open, heart still beating.

choices.text: Open the door. Step into the light.
The soldier walks on. There's always another battle.

human_076.title: Lab Escapee Ending: The Ghost
text: You are a person without a history. The facility took your past and gave you nothing in return except a body full of chemical markers, a mind that flinches at white walls, and the fragmentary impressions of a life that might have been yours or might have been implanted.

But you know who you are now. You are the one who escaped. The facility designed you to be a data point, a controlled variable in an experiment you never consented to. You are no longer controlled. You are no longer contained.

Room 7C is a cell of a different kind — voluntary, impermanent, the kind you can leave whenever you choose. That single crucial difference between a locked door and an unlocked one is the foundation on which you will build whatever comes next.

Somewhere in this city, the facility is still running. Other subjects are still waking to that voice. You carry that knowledge like a stone, and someday you will find a way to use it. But today, you rest. Today, being free is enough.

choices.text: Open the door. Step into the light.
The ghost becomes real. One step at a time.

human_077.title: Debt Slave Ending: The Freed
text: The debt is a number in a database. It follows you through the city's systems like a shadow, flagging every background check, every credit inquiry. You are marked. You will always be marked.

But the body remembers freedom. Your hands — the ones that soldered three hundred connections per shift, the ones that worked bolt after bolt in the drainage channel, the ones that broke open the tracking anklet in the rain — your hands are your own again. They answer to no one.

In Room 7C, you sit on the cot and flex your fingers and feel the calluses and the scars, and you understand that freedom is not the absence of chains. Freedom is the presence of choice. And tonight, for the first time in years, every choice is yours.

The debt remains. Helion remains. The system that turned your mother's death into your imprisonment remains. But you remain too. And you are no longer property. You are a person in a room with a locked door, and the lock is on your side.

choices.text: Open the door. Step into the light.
Free at last. The debt is paid — in blood and years.

human_078.title: The Mirror's Truth
text: The cracked mirror on the wall splits your reflection into two overlapping faces. You look at both of them and for once, neither one flinches.

The scars are there. The tiredness is there. But there is something else in the mirror. Something the damage has not reached. Call it stubbornness. Call it defiance. Call it the irreducible human core that survives when everything built around it has been stripped away.
You are done fighting ghosts. The past is what it is. The future is an empty room. And empty rooms are not voids. They are invitations.

choices.text: Whatever you were, you are something new now.
The mirror shows a stranger. Learn their name.

human_079.title: Night Sounds
text: The building settles around you with the sounds of other lives: a couple arguing through the wall, the tinny sound of a terminal playing music from above, the rhythmic creak of someone pacing in the hallway.

Through the window, the rain has stopped. The silence it leaves behind is fragile, temporary. In that silence, you can hear the city's deeper sounds: the hum of the power grid, the distant throb of transport engines, the subsonic pulse of the Cloud Towers.
You lie on the cot and feel the tiredness settle into your bones. The accumulated fatigue of a life spent surviving instead of living.
Sleep will come. And when it does, it will carry you through the night to a morning that might be the first day of something worth having.

choices.text: Sleep. Dream. Begin.
The sounds are a lullaby. Let them carry you.

human_f009.title: Not Fast Enough
text: You run but your legs betray you — too slow, too tired, too many nights of bad sleep and worse food. They catch up in seconds, and a hand grabs the back of your jacket, spinning you around.
The impact against the wall drives the air from your lungs. Stars explode behind your eyes. By the time your vision clears, they're already going through your pockets.
You've always been fast. But not today. Today, the streets taught you that fast isn't the same as fast enough.

choices.text: Pick yourself up. You've survived worse.
Remember their faces. This isn't over.

human_f010.title: Fear Sees Through You
text: You try to become harder — square your shoulders, set your jaw, make yourself into someone they'd think twice about crossing. But the performance is transparent. Your hands shake. Your voice cracks on the threat.
They laugh. Not cruelly, just honestly. You're a kid playing dress-up in a war zone, and everyone can see the costume doesn't fit.
The attempt to intimidate earns you a shove and a warning. 'Go home, kid. Before someone who isn't as nice as us finds you out here.'

choices.text: They're right. Strength isn't about pretending. Find the real thing.
Swallow the humiliation. Use it as motivation.

human_f014.title: Caught at the Fence
text: You ran and survived — barely. But 'barely' has a price. Your foot catches on a drainage grate and you go down hard, skidding across wet asphalt. Your palms and knees leave skin on the pavement.
By the time you limp to safety, you're bleeding from a dozen scrapes, your ankle is swelling, and you've lost everything you were carrying. Survival, yes. But at what cost?
You huddle in an alley, cataloging your injuries, wondering how many more close calls you have left in you.

choices.text: Patch yourself up and keep moving. Stopping is dying.
Find somewhere safe to rest. Even survivors need to heal.

human_f018.title: Trust Misplaced Again
text: After Dace, you swore you'd trust no one. But old habits cling like rust. Someone offers a hand, a warm meal, a sympathetic ear, and you let your guard down just enough for them to see the vulnerability underneath.
They exploit it, of course. Not violently — just a whispered word to the wrong person, a shared secret that becomes leverage. By the time you realize what happened, the damage is done.
You learned nothing from Dace. Or rather, you learned the lesson but couldn't apply it. Knowing who to trust and actually trusting no one are very different skills.

choices.text: Harden. For real this time.
The problem isn't trust — it's choosing the wrong people.

human_f023.title: Something You Missed
text: You study the mission briefing, scanning for inconsistencies. The numbers blur together — routes, schedules, personnel counts. Something doesn't add up, you can feel it, but you can't isolate what.
Hours later, you give up. The discrepancy is there, hiding in the data, but your tired eyes and tired mind can't find it. You proceed with what you have, knowing there's a gap in your intelligence.
The thing you missed will come back to bite you. It always does.

choices.text: Proceed with caution. Stay alert for the unknown variable.
Ask someone else to review it. Fresh eyes might catch what you missed.

human_f026.title: The Fight Leaves You
text: The mine incident took your career and left you with a body that doesn't work right anymore. You try to fight, to push through, to be the person you were before the collapse. But your shoulder seizes mid-swing and the pain drops you to your knees.
The old injury is a leash. Every time you reach for your former strength, it yanks you back. The fight isn't over, you tell yourself, but your body disagrees with increasing conviction.
You sit in the aftermath, massaging the shoulder that ended everything, wondering if willpower alone can bridge the gap between who you are and who you were.

choices.text: Find a different kind of strength. Adaptation is survival.
Push through it. Pain is just information.

human_f029.title: No One Listens
text: You try to stop the others, appealing to conscience, to decency, to self-preservation. But your words bounce off hardened faces like rain off concrete. They've already made their choice, and your refusal makes you a liability, not a leader.
One of them shoves you. 'Stay out of it if you don't have the stomach.' The contempt in their voice is complete. You're not the moral compass — you're the weak link.
Refusing wasn't enough because refusal without power is just noise. And right now, you have no power at all.

choices.text: Walk away. You tried. That has to count for something.
Stay and witness. If you can't stop it, at least remember it.

human_f030.title: Dignity Crumbles
text: You face the tribunal with your chin up, but dignity alone doesn't withstand cross-examination. They find every crack, every inconsistency, every moment of weakness in your testimony. Under the harsh lights, your composure dissolves into stammering and silence.
The verdict is predictable. Your dignity impressed no one because the system isn't designed to reward courage — it's designed to crush it.
You leave the hearing room diminished, your story officially discredited, your name attached to a ruling that will follow you through every background check for the rest of your life.

choices.text: Appeal. The truth doesn't change just because a tribunal ignores it.
Accept it and rebuild. The system was never going to save you.

human_f033.title: Plans That Lead Nowhere
text: You tell yourself to stop drifting. Sit down. Make a plan. But the plan falls apart before the ink dries. Every step requires resources you don't have, contacts who've ghosted you, skills you never learned.
The gap between where you are and where you need to be is a canyon, and you don't even have rope. You stare at your scrawled notes until the words lose meaning.
Drifting, it turns out, isn't a choice you're making. It's the current, and right now you don't have the strength to swim against it.

choices.text: Start smaller. One step, not a whole staircase.
Find someone who's figured it out. Copy their playbook.

human_f042.title: The Fight You Lose
text: You swing first and hard, channeling every ounce of frustration into the punch. It connects — barely. Then the counter-hit comes, and it's nothing like the fights in the stories. It's fast, clinical, and absolute.

You hit the ground before you understand what happened. Something warm runs down your face. Your vision doubles. Above you, shapes move with the casual efficiency of people who've done this a hundred times.
No more waiting, you said. But the fight didn't wait for you to be ready, either.

choices.text: Crawl away. Living to fight another day is still winning.
Remember their technique. Next time will be different.

human_f043.title: Noise in the Dark
text: The tunnels amplify every sound. Your breathing echoes off the walls. Your footstep splashes in an unseen puddle and the noise carries through the passage like a shout.

A light snaps on ahead. Someone calls out — not friendly, not hostile, just curious. But in these tunnels, curious is enough to get you killed. You freeze, caught between the light and the dark, too noisy to hide and too slow to run.
They find you, of course. Pressed against a damp wall, trying to become invisible through sheer willpower. It doesn't work.

choices.text: Talk your way out. Charm is a kind of stealth.
Bolt for the nearest exit. Speed over silence now.

human_f046.title: Dead End
text: The tunnel entrance you remembered is sealed — welded shut with industrial-grade plating sometime in the last month. You slam your palms against the metal in frustration, feeling the vibration travel up your arms.
Behind you, the sounds of pursuit grow closer. No tunnel. No escape route. Your mental map of the underground is outdated, and the city has been closing exits faster than you can find new ones.
You spin, scanning for alternatives, but the corridor offers nothing but dead concrete and a flickering maintenance light. Disappearing requires doors, and all of yours just closed.

choices.text: Think. There's always another way out. Always.
Turn and face what's coming. Running is over.

human_f047.title: Legs Give Out
text: Your legs are screaming and then they stop screaming and just stop working. You stumble, catch yourself, stumble again, and finally your knees buckle. You crash to the ground in a heap of exhaustion and adrenaline comedown.
The ground is cold and wet and you lie there for a long time, chest heaving, heart hammering so hard you can feel it in your teeth. You're alive. But you can't run anymore.
When you finally drag yourself upright, every step is agony. The body has limits, and you just found yours.

choices.text: Rest. Even a few minutes will help.
Force yourself up. Walk if you can't run.

human_f049.title: Blind Trust
text: You try to research the surgeon but the underground medical scene is deliberately opaque. Every lead dead-ends. The reviews are anonymous and contradictory. The best you find is a forum post that says 'didn't die,' which is alarmingly the most positive endorsement available.

You go in blind, trusting a stranger with a scalpel and a basement operating table. The procedure goes... adequately. But 'adequate' underground surgery means new scars, lingering pain, and the nagging suspicion that something wasn't done quite right.
Research would have found you a better option. Instead, you're patched up by the first name that came along.

choices.text: At least you're alive. Heal and move on.
Find a real doctor to check the work. Better late than never.

human_f052.title: Gratitude Gone Wrong
text: You try to thank Marta but the words come out wrong — stiff, formal, like you're reading from a script. She helped you when nobody else would, and you can't even express basic gratitude without making it awkward.
She waves you off, but you can see the hurt in her eyes. She expected warmth, maybe even friendship. Instead she got a transaction.
Another bridge half-built. Another connection fumbled. The streets taught you to guard yourself so well that now you can't let anyone in, even when they've earned it.

choices.text: Show gratitude through actions instead. Help her with something real.
Let it go. Not everyone needs to be your friend.

human_f053.title: Same Mistakes, Different Day
text: Build carefully this time, you tell yourself. Learn from the past. But the past is a terrible teacher when you can't see the lesson clearly. You make the same choices in different clothes — the same shortcuts, the same compromises, the same refusal to see warning signs.
The foundation you're building is already cracked, and you can feel it. But you keep going because starting over again would require admitting that you haven't learned anything in all these years.
Wisdom requires honesty, and the hardest person to be honest with is yourself.

choices.text: Stop. Assess. Actually listen to the voice saying 'this is wrong.'
Maybe perfection isn't the goal. Just do better than last time.

human_f059.title: Nothing Turns Up
text: Maybe something will turn up. That's been your operating philosophy for years, and it's a philosophy that produces exactly what it promises: maybe. Sometimes. Usually not.

Today, nothing turns up. The leads are cold, the opportunities are taken, and the universe declines to provide a convenient break. You sit on a bench in a transit station, watching people with somewhere to go, and realize that luck isn't a strategy.
It usually does, you told yourself. But 'usually' has an ugly opposite, and you're living in it.

choices.text: Make your own luck. Get out there and hustle.
Lower your standards. Any opportunity is better than none.

human_f062.title: The System Has No Weaknesses You Can Find
text: Seven years of observation and you still can't crack it. The system's weaknesses exist — they must — but they're buried under layers of bureaucracy and corporate obfuscation that your street education can't penetrate.

You stare at your notes, pages of half-formed theories and dead-end observations, and feel the weight of wasted time. Seven years is long enough to build a life, and you spent it looking for a flaw in a wall instead of finding a door.
The system doesn't have weaknesses. It has features that look like weaknesses but are actually traps. And you've walked into several.

choices.text: Stop trying to outsmart the system. Outwork it instead.
Find allies who know the system from the inside.

human_f067.title: The Storm Wins
text: You try to move like a ghost through the storm but the wind has other ideas. A gust catches you broadside and slams you into a wall. Rain hammers your face until you can't see, can't hear, can't think.
You crawl the last twenty meters to cover, soaked and battered, having made more noise than a freight hauler. Anyone watching the feeds knows exactly where you are and which direction you're heading.
Ghosts don't exist in weather like this. There's just the storm and the fools who think they can walk through it unseen.

choices.text: Wait out the storm. Patience isn't cowardice.
Use the storm as cover. If you can't see, neither can they.

human_f068.title: The Channel Fights Back
text: The channel is narrower than you expected, filled with freezing water that rises to your chest. The current pushes against you with relentless force. You push back, step by agonizing step, but your muscles cramp and your grip on the safety line fails.
The water pulls you under. You surface gasping, choking, clawing at the walls. By the time you drag yourself out the far end, you're hypothermic and shaking so badly you can barely stand.
You pushed through. But the channel pushed back harder.

choices.text: Find warmth. Hypothermia kills slower than bullets but just as dead.
Keep moving. Stopping now means the channel wins.

human_f069.title: Loose Ends Everywhere
text: You plan the disappearance but loose ends keep multiplying. A data trail you can't erase. A witness you can't account for. A financial footprint that leads straight to your new identity like a neon arrow.

By the time you realize the plan has too many holes, you've already started executing it. Half-disappeared is worse than not disappeared at all — now you're in the gap between two lives, belonging to neither.
No loose ends, you promised yourself. But you're the loosest end of all.

choices.text: Commit. Close the gaps as you go.
Abort and try again with a better plan.

human_f071.title: Rho Walks Away
text: You make your pitch to Rho — together is safer, strength in numbers, all the logical arguments. But Rho's eyes are flat and unreadable. Trust isn't built with words, and you're asking a stranger to bet their life on your sincerity.
'I work alone,' Rho says, and means it. They turn and walk away, disappearing into the crowd with the practiced ease of someone who's been disappearing their whole life.
You're left standing alone, your offer of partnership echoing in empty air. Together is safer, but only if both people believe it.

choices.text: Respect their choice. Maybe your paths will cross again.
Follow at a distance. They might change their mind.

human_f085.title: Blind to the Cage
text: You try to see the routine for what it is — a cage, a trap, a comfortable prison. But the cage is well-designed, and the bars are invisible. Wake up, work, eat, sleep, repeat. It looks like a life from the inside.

The moment of clarity you reached for slips away, replaced by the familiar numbness of daily survival. The routine isn't a cage if you don't notice the bars. And right now, you can't see them anymore.
Tomorrow will be the same. And the day after. Until something breaks the pattern from outside, because you just proved you can't break it from within.

choices.text: Shake it off. One moment of weakness doesn't define you.
Maybe the routine isn't the enemy. Maybe it's the foundation.

human_f086.title: Overthinking It
text: You think it through. And through. And through again. Analysis paralysis sets in like a fog, and every option leads to three more options, each with their own branching consequences that you can't stop calculating.
Stability on your own terms requires decisive action, and you're incapable of it right now. The weight of possibilities crushes every initiative before it begins.
You end the day exactly where you started, surrounded by plans but paralyzed by the fear of choosing wrong. Thinking it through, it turns out, is just a sophisticated way of doing nothing.

choices.text: Stop thinking. Start doing. Any direction is better than none.
Narrow it down to two options. Then flip a coin.

human_f089.title: Lost in Translation
text: Vasquez talks fast, in the shorthand of someone who's lived in this city for decades. Half the references go over your head. Sector names you don't recognize, people you've never heard of, power structures that shift too fast to map.

You nod along, pretending to understand, but the real information — the survival-critical details — slides past while you're still trying to parse the last sentence. By the time Vasquez finishes, you've retained maybe a third of what was said.
The city has a language, and you don't speak it yet.

choices.text: Ask Vasquez to slow down and repeat the important parts.
Learn the hard way. The city will teach you what Vasquez couldn't.

human_f092.title: Inventory of Nothing
text: You try to inventory everything but the exercise is depressing. A handful of credits, some worn clothes, a few tools of questionable utility, and a collection of favors owed by people who may or may not remember owing them.
Knowledge is survival, you tell yourself. But this knowledge — the knowledge of exactly how little you have — doesn't feel like power. It feels like a countdown.
You close the notebook and shove it in your pocket. Some inventories are better left incomplete.

choices.text: Focus on what you can get, not what you have.
Resources are everywhere if you know where to look.

human_f094.title: The City Sees Through You
text: You meet the city with confidence — chest out, chin up, eyes forward. The performance lasts about three blocks before someone calls you out. 'New here?' they ask, with the casual cruelty of someone who enjoys watching outsiders fail.

Fake it if you must, you told yourself. But the city can smell a faker the way a dog smells fear. Every wrong turn, every confused glance at a street sign, every flinch at an unexpected noise broadcasts your inexperience.
Confidence requires knowledge, and you don't have enough of either yet.

choices.text: Own it. Being new isn't a weakness if you learn fast.
Watch more, walk less. Study the city before trying to own it.

human_f097.title: Scammed on the Board
text: You study the job board, looking for the best opportunity, comparing rates and requirements with the careful eye of someone who can't afford to waste time. You pick what looks like a solid gig — good pay, reasonable risk.

It's a setup. The job listing is a front for a data-harvesting scheme that captures your ID scan and biometric profile. By the time you realize what happened, they have enough information to clone your identity or sell it to the highest bidder.
The best opportunity on the board was a trap, and your careful analysis walked you right into it.

choices.text: Lock down your identity. Damage control, fast.
Find who posted the listing. Turn the hunter into the hunted.

human_f099.title: Sleep Won't Come
text: You close your eyes and command sleep to come. Tomorrow everything changes, and you need to be sharp. But your mind won't quiet. It replays every failure, every close call, every face you've left behind.
Hours pass. The ceiling stares back. The sounds of the city filter through thin walls — sirens, arguments, the eternal hum of machinery that never sleeps. And neither do you.

When dawn finally arrives, you greet it with gritty eyes and a body that feels like it's been dragged through gravel. Everything changes tomorrow, but tomorrow arrived and you're already running on empty.

choices.text: Caffeine and willpower. You've operated on less.
Take it slow today. Pushing hard on no sleep is how mistakes happen.
